bills.title: CA AB1642 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, known as the Wildfire Environmental Safety and Testing Act, aims to establish clearer standards for reoccupying homes, schools, and workplaces after wildfires by creating a nonrebuttable presumption that a structure is safe if it meets specific lead and TEM-identified asbestos (Transmission Electron Microscopy-identified asbestos) levels. The Department of Toxic Substances Control (Department) is tasked with adopting emergency regulations by July 1, 2027, outlining science-informed, health-based standards for investigating, testing, and clearing lead and asbestos contamination in residential areas after a wildfire. Furthermore, by July 1, 2028, the Department, in consultation with the Office of Environmental Health Hazard Assessment (Office), must adopt regulations for hazardous chemicals, setting levels that ensure safe reoccupancy and prevent new cancer cases caused by wildfire contamination. The bill also defines key terms like "downwind ash zone," which is the area downwind of a wildfire likely to have received ash and debris, and requires the Department to determine this zone using data from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. This legislation addresses the current lack of uniform statewide standards for post-wildfire habitability and reoccupancy, which can leave residents exposed to serious health risks.

bills.title: CA AB1666 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the Biomass Innovation Parks Act, establishes a program to create designated "biomass innovation parks" across California to process wood waste from forests and agriculture. These parks will be overseen by the Natural Resources Agency, in collaboration with the Department of Food and Agriculture and CAL-FIRE (California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection). Key provisions require that any wood waste processed at these parks must originate in California and be converted into carbon-beneficial products using non-combustion technologies, such as creating wood products or producing biomethane or clean hydrogen, rather than burning it. The parks must also implement community benefits programs and prioritize projects developed with or by California Native American tribes. The bill also mandates regional assessments of wood waste availability, a system to track the origin of forest biomass, and encourages other state agencies to promote the use of California-produced wood products in housing and building standards, with a portion of funds from the Safe Drinking Water, Wildfire Prevention, Drought Preparedness, and Clean Air Bond Act of 2024 being allocated to support this program.

bills.title: CA AB706 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the Forest Organic Residue, Energy, and Safety Transformation and Wildfire Prevention Fund Act, aims to address California's wildfire crisis and promote renewable energy by establishing a new program and fund. It proposes to create the Forest Organic Residue Energy and Safety Transformation (FOREST) program, which will use money from the Timber Regulation and Forest Restoration Fund or the Greenhouse Gas Reduction Fund to support and expand biomass power generation. This includes revitalizing existing facilities and creating new capacity for using forest biomass waste, which is defined as byproducts from forest management for wildfire mitigation, prevention, restoration, or public safety. The bill also establishes a fire fuel reduction program to procure, transport, and beneficially use up to 15,000,000 bone-dry tons of forest biomass waste annually, thereby reducing wildfire fuel. To support these efforts, a FOREST and Wildfire Prevention Fund will be created in the State Treasury, with funds available for appropriation to the Natural Resources Agency for these programs. The bill prioritizes funding for existing BioRAM (Bioenergy Renewable Auction Mechanism) and BioMAT (Bioenergy Market Adjusting Tariff) facilities, which are programs designed to incentivize bioenergy projects, particularly those using forest biomass from high-hazard zones. Importantly, the bill clarifies that ratepayer funds will not be used to finance the FOREST fund.

bills.title: CA SB894 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the California Wildfire Resilience Loan Program, to be administered by the California Alternative Energy and Advanced Transportation Financing Authority (CAEATFA), a state agency that provides financing for energy and transportation projects. The program aims to reduce wildfire losses and improve community resilience by providing financial assistance for projects like home hardening, which involves making homes more resistant to fire, and creating defensible space around properties, which is cleared land around a structure to slow the spread of fire. The bill also clarifies that the CAEATFA's existing authority to promote wildfire risk reduction investments is essential for public safety and economic stability, and it expands the types of facilities and projects that the authority can finance to include those intended to reduce wildfire risk and enhance community resilience. The program will be funded through legislative appropriations, including any federal or private funds received for this purpose, and can offer tools like loan loss reserves and interest rate buy-downs to make wildfire resilience improvements more affordable and accessible to eligible property owners, including individuals, small businesses, and non-profits. The bill also allows the CAEATFA to partner with various entities to identify applicants and facilitate projects, and to establish procedures for verifying improvements and creating a public website for the program.

bills.title: CA SB973 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Wildfire County Coordinator Program within the Department of Forestry and Fire Protection to enhance local capacity for wildfire mitigation, prevention, preparedness, and recovery. The Department will develop recommended standards and guidance for wildfire risk modeling and analysis tools, create tools for wildfire risk assessments, and support the development or updating of county-level risk assessments. It will also maintain oversight of the program and develop guidance for county wildfire resilience plans, ensuring they integrate with existing state and local efforts. The bill mandates that the Department partner with the California Fire Safe Council (CFSC), a non-profit organization that provides wildfire preparedness support, to administer and deliver the program. The program's goals include building capacity in participating counties to execute wildfire resilience activities aligned with the state's Wildfire and Forest Resilience Action Plan, facilitating local coordination with various partners, and collecting data on mitigation efforts and their outcomes. Additionally, the program will support the use of risk assessment tools, disseminate educational materials on wildfire and smoke mitigation, provide information on funding resources, and assist in planning and implementing home hardening and defensible space projects, including strategies to reduce costs and develop a list of mitigation practitioners. The program will also coordinate with other entities to improve access to financial assistance for home hardening and defensible space and support community resilience to smoke.

bills.title: CA AB2038 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, an amendment to Section 675.1 of the Insurance Code, aims to strengthen protections for homeowners whose residential property insurance policies might be canceled or not renewed after a wildfire. Specifically, it extends the period during which an insurer must renew a policy following a total loss to the primary insured structure caused by a disaster from two annual renewal periods (or 24 months) to three annual renewal periods (or 36 months). Additionally, the bill doubles the duration that insurers are prohibited from canceling or refusing to renew a policy for properties located in ZIP codes within or adjacent to a fire perimeter, extending this moratorium from one year to two years after a state of emergency is declared due to a wildfire. The bill also makes technical, nonsubstantive changes to provisions regarding crop insurance adjuster licenses.

bills.title: CA SB1370 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill aims to streamline and expedite the review process for "covered wildfire mitigation projects," which are activities like removing hazardous vegetation, creating fuel breaks, or clearing around utility lines to reduce wildfire risk. It establishes a "consolidated review" process administered jointly by the Natural Resources Agency and the California Environmental Protection Agency, referred to as the "consolidated review agencies." Applicants will submit a single application package, and these agencies must issue a determination within 60 days, with provisions for extensions if more information is needed. This expedited authorization will replace other state permits and approvals for these projects, and importantly, exempts them from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), which normally requires environmental impact reviews. Projects must be under 3,000 acres and completed within two years of approval. The bill also mandates that the administrator of the Wildfire Fund present recommendations from a report on natural catastrophe mitigation to specific Senate committees.

bills.title: CA AB69 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill aims to improve how the California FAIR Plan, which provides basic property insurance to those who can't get it elsewhere, connects policyholders with insurers in the voluntary market. Starting January 1, 2028, the FAIR Plan will be allowed to share policyholder information with insurers participating in its "clearinghouse program," a system designed to help FAIR Plan policyholders find coverage from regular insurance companies. If a policy has an agent or broker listed, insurers must offer policies to both the agent/broker and the policyholder at the same time. Additionally, starting May 1, 2027, participating insurers must report how many FAIR Plan policyholders they've insured through this program to the FAIR Plan, which will then share aggregated data publicly and with lawmakers. The FAIR Plan will also be required to send policyholders annual notices about their coverage options, including with their initial policy and upon renewal, and agents and brokers must inform people about these clearinghouse programs and complete training on advising policyholders about voluntary market options.

bills.title: CA AB306 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill expands the California Building Standards Commission's (CBSC) authority to hear appeals and issue interpretations regarding building standards, aiming to streamline housing development and ensure consistent application of codes statewide. It allows individuals or local agencies to appeal to the CBSC if they are adversely affected by building standards, local amendments, or interpretations of the California Building Standards Code, provided the issue has potential statewide significance, such as impacting housing delivery or affordability, and removes the previous requirement for both parties to agree to an appeal. The bill also introduces a process for requesting "code equivalence determinations," where the CBSC can officially determine if an alternative material, design, or construction method meets or exceeds the requirements of the California Building Standards Code, with these determinations also needing to demonstrate potential statewide significance. Furthermore, it allows the CBSC to designate certain appeal decisions and code equivalence determinations as "precedent decisions," which would then be binding on state and local agencies to promote uniformity, and requires local agencies to post their interpretations of the building code online. The bill also clarifies that appeals to the CBSC may not require exhausting all local appeal procedures if the issue has statewide significance, and prohibits local agencies from penalizing permit applicants for participating in such appeals.

bills.title: CA SB876 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, concerning fire and residential property insurance, aims to strengthen consumer protections and improve insurer responsiveness, particularly during states of emergency. Key provisions include requiring insurers to cover building code upgrade costs for rebuilding at a new location or purchasing an existing home, and mandating that building code upgrade coverage be at least 20% of policy limits during a state of emergency. It also mandates that insurers offer extended replacement cost coverage, which provides additional coverage beyond the policy limits, to homeowners, requiring a signed acknowledgment if declined. The bill enhances requirements for insurers to provide cost estimates for rebuilding and clarifies that additional living expenses (ALE) coverage must cover all reasonable additional expenses to maintain a comparable standard of living, allowing policyholders to opt for the dwelling's fair rental value instead of itemized expenses. Furthermore, it extends ALE coverage by 15 days after a property is deemed habitable following a total loss during a state of emergency and requires insurers to assign a primary claims adjuster point of contact within 30 days of a loss during a state of emergency, with stricter reporting timelines for adjuster changes. The bill also increases penalties for unfair insurance practices during states of emergency, requires insurers to submit disaster response plans, and mandates reporting of losses and estimated incurred losses to the Department of Insurance within 30 days of a declared state of emergency. Finally, it adjusts reporting thresholds for insurers regarding residential property experience data and clarifies the definition of building code upgrade coverage to apply to codes in effect at the time of rebuilding.

bills.title: CA AB1554 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires the California Earthquake Authority (CEA), which provides earthquake insurance, to send its annual report on program operations and financial condition not only to the Legislature and the Insurance Commissioner but also specifically to the Senate Committee on Insurance and the Assembly Committee on Insurance, and ensures that the certified examination and verification report of the CEA's operations is posted on its website. Additionally, the bill mandates that a working group, established to recommend market mechanisms for investing in natural infrastructure to reduce climate change risks, must incorporate hazard mitigation projects into its recommendations by January 1, 2028.

bills.title: CA AB1680 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the California FAIR Plan Association, which is a program where insurance companies collectively provide basic property insurance to those who can't get it elsewhere. The bill requires the FAIR Plan Association to take specific corrective actions when it violates laws, regulations, accounting principles, or its own operational rules, as identified by the Insurance Commissioner or their designee. If the association fails to make these corrections within an agreed-upon timeframe, it can be fined up to $20,000 for each failure. Additionally, the bill establishes new civil penalties for violations related to the association, with fines up to $10,000 for each violation, or up to $20,000 if the violation was willful. The Insurance Commissioner will be responsible for imposing these penalties. Furthermore, the bill grants the commissioner the authority to require the association to adjust policy limits and offer additional coverage options, such as fair rental value coverage, within its renters' property insurance program.

bills.title: CA AB1795 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, known as the Smoke Damage Recovery Act, aims to establish clear standards and procedures for handling smoke damage claims from wildfires. It mandates that the California Environmental Protection Agency (CalEPA) develop health-based standards for testing and screening residential properties affected by wildfire smoke by June 30, 2027, and establish training and certification requirements for professionals involved in smoke damage inspection, testing, and restoration by January 1, 2028. The bill also requires insurers to continue providing additional living expenses (ALE) coverage until a property is cleared for habitation, cover the costs of required sampling and testing, and adhere to specific timelines for property inspections and payments for restoration. Furthermore, it directs the Department of Insurance to develop training and certification programs for insurance adjusters and public insurance adjusters who handle smoke damage claims, ensuring they are qualified to assess such damages. The bill declares an urgency to take effect immediately, citing the need to protect public health and safety by ensuring properties are properly restored and habitable after wildfire smoke damage.

bills.title: CA AB2061 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires the California FAIR Plan Association, which provides basic property insurance to those unable to obtain it through normal channels, to publicly share more data on its website each quarter, specifically the number of policies issued in each ZIP Code and the association's overall risk exposure. The bill also removes the requirement for the association to list its toll-free number in all general telephone directories, while still requiring it to be available on their website and in communications.

bills.title: CA SB1301 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, effective January 1, 2028, significantly alters how residential property insurance nonrenewals and coverage changes are handled by insurers in California. It mandates that insurers provide policyholders with at least 90 days' notice before a policy expires if they intend to nonrenew, reduce coverage limits, or eliminate coverage, requiring a detailed, plain-language explanation of the reasons, including specific underwriting guidelines and any relevant documentation like nonaerial imagery. If a policyholder can remedy a condition that violates underwriting guidelines, the insurer must provide at least 120 days' notice detailing the necessary remediation and offer the policyholder at least 90 days, with a possible 90-day extension, to make those changes, with the insurer covering the cost of any necessary inspection to verify the remediation. Furthermore, insurers are prohibited from nonrenewing or denying coverage based on certain types of claims, previous inquiries about the policy, or the age of the roof if an independent inspection shows at least five years of remaining useful life. Insurers will also be required to report annually to the Department of Insurance on nonrenewal and coverage reduction data, which the department will then publish.

bills.title: CA AB2724 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, building on existing regulations for insurance availability and market stability, aims to refine how "distressed areas" are identified for the purpose of using catastrophe modeling, particularly for properties exposed to wildfire risk. Currently, a distressed area can include undermarketed ZIP Codes and distressed counties, but this bill proposes to limit it to only undermarketed ZIP Codes. An undermarketed ZIP Code is defined as one where at least 10% of residential properties are insured by the FAIR Plan (California FAIR Plan Association, which provides basic property insurance when it cannot be obtained through normal channels) when considering both FAIR Plan and voluntary market insurance. The bill mandates that the Department of Insurance, starting July 1, 2027, and annually thereafter, must review and update these distressed areas, which will include consulting with the Department of Forestry and Fire Protection and holding public meetings to gather suggestions. By January 1, 2028, and annually thereafter, the department will publish a list of these ZIP Codes on its website and provide it to legislative committees.

bills.title: CA AB2513 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill aims to enhance California's wildfire resilience by expanding and clarifying existing programs. It authorizes the Director of the Department of Conservation to directly award "regional landscape grants" to regional entities, which are defined as organizations with the capacity to coordinate regional partners, to implement strategies for creating fire-adapted communities and landscapes. These grants will be guided by established funding guidelines that align with California's Wildfire and Forest Resilience Action Plan, a comprehensive strategy to address wildfire threats. The bill also extends the ability for advance grant payments indefinitely and expands eligible activities under the local assistance grant program for fire prevention and home hardening education to include broader vegetation modification along roadways and driveways, specifically addressing wildfire ignition risk and adding "ignition prevention" as an eligible activity. Furthermore, it allows the Wildlife Conservation Board to award similar regional landscape grants to local entities, also requiring adherence to guidelines aligned with the state's wildfire resilience plan. Finally, the bill clarifies definitions related to "flashy fuels" and "nonnative fuels" to better categorize and manage vegetation that contributes to rapid fire spread.

bills.title: RI H7865 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the "Strengthen Rhody Homes" program within the Department of Business Regulation, Division of Insurance, to help fund home hardening projects that make residential properties more resilient to hurricanes and other catastrophic weather events. The program is designed to seek and receive federal grants or funds from other sources, as it does not create an entitlement for property owners or obligate the state to provide funding. Eligible homeowners must meet specific criteria, including owning and occupying a single-family primary residence that is in good repair (unless recently damaged by a catastrophic event), and the home must be prequalified by a certified evaluator from the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety (IBHS) for improvements to meet IBHS FORTIFIED standards. Homeowners will be responsible for hiring IBHS-certified contractors and paying evaluator fees, and grant funds will be paid directly to contractors upon completion and certification of the work. The program will prioritize lower-income applicants and those in areas with higher susceptibility to weather events, and all mitigation work must adhere to local building codes and secure necessary permits. The bill also outlines requirements for contractors and evaluators, including certifications and avoiding conflicts of interest, and establishes a "Strengthen Rhody Homes Revolving Fund" to hold and disburse grant monies.

bills.title: RI S3150 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the "Strengthen Rhody Homes" program within the department of business regulation's division of insurance, creating a framework for the state to seek and receive grants, particularly from federal sources, to fund home hardening projects. Home hardening refers to making residential properties more resilient to damage from hurricanes or other catastrophic weather events. The program is not an entitlement and is contingent on securing external funding, with the insurance superintendent tasked with actively pursuing grants. Homeowners can receive grants to construct or retrofit their primary, owner-occupied single-family homes to meet specific standards, such as those set by the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety (IBHS) Fortified Homes Program, which involves prequalification by a certified evaluator and work by certified contractors. The bill also outlines eligibility requirements for homeowners and contractors, including proof of wind and, if applicable, flood insurance, and specifies that grant funds will be paid directly to contractors after a project is certified. A dedicated "Strengthen Rhody Homes Revolving Fund" will be created to manage these grant monies, which will be appropriated for the program's purposes and will not lapse. The bill also allows for grants to be made to nonprofit entities for similar projects under certain conditions and emphasizes that all mitigation work must comply with local building codes and secure necessary permits and inspections.

bills.title: IL SB4006 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill aims to enhance climate risk awareness and strengthen home resilience by amending various Illinois laws. It establishes a "Climate Risk Disclosure Law" requiring companies, corporations, and organizations that write substantial amounts of insurance annually to participate in climate surveys coordinated by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC), a body that helps regulate insurance at the state level. Additionally, it introduces the "Strengthen Illinois Homes Act," which will create a pilot grant program to help homeowners make their properties more resilient, and mandates that insurers provide actuarially justified premium discounts or rate reductions for homeowners insurance policies on properties meeting certain mitigation standards, and requires insurers to offer optional riders for claim payments to upgrade non-fortified properties to these standards. The bill also mandates that insurers providing fire and extended coverage that *doesn't* include flood coverage must clearly inform policyholders about this exclusion and the availability of flood insurance through the National Flood Insurance Program, requiring a signed acknowledgment from the policyholder. Furthermore, it extends the required notice period for insurers intending not to renew certain policies from 30 to 60 days, and updates other related insurance notification requirements.

bills.title: RI S2011 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires homeowner's insurance providers to give policyholders at least sixty (60) days' written notice before non-renewal of their policy and at least thirty (30) days' written notice before any reduction in coverage, elimination of coverage, or increase in deductibles that the policyholder did not request, with a violation of these notice requirements being considered a deceptive trade practice punishable by a fine of up to $1,000, and these new provisions will go into effect on January 1, 2027.

bills.title: RI H7066 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires homeowner's insurance providers to give policyholders at least sixty (60) days' notice before a policy renewal premium increases by more than 20%, or if there are any reductions or eliminations in coverage, or increased deductibles that the policyholder did not request. This change, which takes effect on July 1, 2027, aims to provide homeowners with more time to understand and potentially find alternative coverage when significant changes occur to their insurance policies. Violating these notice requirements will be considered a deceptive trade practice, subject to a fine of up to $1,000.

bills.title: CA SB877 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the definition of "claim-related documents" within residential property insurance policies to include all documents, calculations, and estimates, whether preliminary or final, that pertain to the amount of loss, covered damage, and cost of repairs. This expansion aims to increase transparency by ensuring that policyholders have access to a broader range of information used by insurers to assess claims. The bill also clarifies that the state will not reimburse local agencies for any costs incurred due to these changes, as they are related to the definition of a crime.

bills.title: CA SB878 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill aims to improve the timeliness and clarity of insurance claim payments, particularly for residential property insurance involving fire damage. It establishes specific deadlines for insurers to acknowledge claims, provide necessary forms and assistance, and begin investigations, generally within 15 days of receiving a claim notice. Insurers must then accept or deny claims within 40 days, or provide written notice of the need for more time, explaining the reasons and any required additional information. Upon accepting a claim, insurers are required to tender payment or take action to fulfill their obligation within 30 days. The bill also clarifies that if a residential structure is a total loss, insurers must pay the actual cash value (the cost to repair or rebuild minus depreciation) within 30 days of determining the total loss, with exceptions for situations where access is restricted or the policyholder is providing necessary information. Furthermore, it mandates that insurers pay the undisputed replacement cost, which is the full cost to repair or rebuild without depreciation, within 30 days after the insured secures a contract for repairs or purchases a replacement home. If these payment deadlines are missed, interest will accrue on the unpaid amounts. The bill also addresses situations involving states of emergency, extending deadlines for collecting full replacement costs and submitting proof of loss, and clarifies that if a policyholder rebuilds or buys a replacement home at a new location, the insurer cannot deduct the value of the land at the new location from the payout, ensuring the policyholder receives the amount they would have recovered had they rebuilt at the original site, up to policy limits.

bills.title: IL HB4273 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends the Business Corporation Act of 1983 to establish clearer rules regarding "corporate opportunities," which are business prospects that a corporation might reasonably be expected to pursue.  Under this new law, individuals in positions of trust within a corporation, such as directors, officers, or key employees, are generally prohibited from personally benefiting from a corporate opportunity unless they first offer it to the corporation. The corporation can then formally reject the opportunity, either through a vote by directors who have no personal stake in the opportunity (disinterested board of directors) or by a vote of shareholders who also have no personal stake (disinterested shareholder action). If the corporation rejects the opportunity through one of these methods, the individual may then pursue it for their own benefit. This provision applies unless the corporation's founding document, the articles of incorporation, states otherwise. The bill becomes effective immediately upon becoming law.

bills.title: NY S08583 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires property and casualty insurance companies to submit detailed, zip code-level data to the Department of Financial Services, including information on nonrenewal and cancellation rates, claim frequency and amounts, paid loss ratios, and average premiums, and mandates that companies using natural disaster risk models disclose those models. It also introduces a premium discount for policyholders who implement property-specific or community-level mitigation actions to reduce risk from natural disasters, with insurers required to clearly communicate these discounts and provide an appeals process for policyholders. Additionally, the bill extends the required notice period for nonrenewal, conditional renewal, or cancellation of certain insurance policies to 90 days and expands the types of discounts insurers must offer for fire prevention, theft prevention, water damage prevention, and wind damage mitigation improvements to residential properties. It also prohibits insurers from canceling, refusing to renew, or increasing premiums for residential buildings in "disadvantaged communities" without providing actuarial justification, increases the number of directors on the board governing the New York Property Insurance Underwriting Association, and requires a quadrennial report on the association's activities.

bills.title: CO SB155 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the "Strengthen Colorado Homes Enterprise" to address challenges in homeowner's insurance availability and affordability, which are exacerbated by increasing extreme weather events like hail, windstorms, and wildfires due to climate change. The enterprise will be funded by a fee of one-half of one percent on insurers offering homeowner's insurance, which insurers cannot pass on to policyholders. This revenue will be used to provide grants to homeowners for "home hardening," meaning making homes more resilient to extreme weather, specifically by encouraging the installation of "resilient roof systems" that meet certain certifications. The enterprise will also set standards for these roof systems, train a workforce to certify them, and develop codes of conduct for roofing contractors. Additionally, insurers will be required to file annual reports detailing policy numbers, the installation of resilient roofs, any discounts applied for these roofs, and claims data related to wind and hail damage for homes with and without resilient roofs. The bill also includes an appropriation for legal services to support the department of regulatory agencies in implementing these changes.

bills.title: NY A09016 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires property and casualty insurance companies to submit detailed zip code-level data to the Department of Financial Services, including information on nonrenewal and cancellation rates, claim frequency and amounts, paid loss ratios, and average premiums, and mandates that companies using natural disaster risk models disclose these models to the state. It also introduces a premium discount for policyholders who implement property-specific or community-level mitigation actions to reduce natural disaster risk, with insurers required to clearly communicate available discounts and provide an appeals process for policyholders if discounts are inaccurately applied. Furthermore, the bill extends the required notice period for nonrenewal, conditional renewal, or cancellation of certain insurance policies to ninety days, and increases the number of directors on the board governing the New York Property Insurance Underwriting Association, requiring a quadrennial report on its activities.

bills.title: NY A09279 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, known as the "climate accountability and loss recovery act," allows the Attorney General to sue certain companies for costs and damages related to climate disasters. A "climate disaster" is defined broadly to include events like fires, floods, and storms, as well as certain declared emergencies, if climate change was a significant factor in their occurrence or severity. A "covered entity" is defined as a company with at least $500 million in annual revenue or market capitalization that is involved in the extraction, production, manufacture, or wholesale sale of "fossil fuel products" (coal, fossil fuels, fuel gases, and petroleum products) and has sufficient ties to New York State. The bill establishes that these covered entities are strictly liable, meaning they can be held responsible regardless of fault, for damages and costs incurred due to climate disasters that are transferred, pooled, assessed, or publicly backstopped through insurance mechanisms like the New York property insurance underwriting association or residual markets. The Attorney General can recover these costs, as well as their own legal expenses, and any other relief deemed proper by a court, though damages will be offset by any amounts already reimbursed to claimants. The bill also amends insurance law to ensure that any money recovered through this act is considered when making insurance rates.

bills.title: LA HCR117 (2026)
bills.summary: This Concurrent Resolution urges and requests the Louisiana State Law Institute, a legal research organization, to establish a Homeowner Protection and Efficiency (HOPE) Committee or advisory group. This committee's primary purpose is to study and propose ways to improve how residential property insurance claims are handled, especially after major disasters, focusing on making the process more efficient, transparent, and fair for homeowners dealing with Louisiana Citizens Property Insurance Corporation (Citizens), which is the state's insurer of last resort. The committee will examine methods to reduce delays and litigation costs, review court procedures used after catastrophes, and explore ways to encourage prompt claim investigations, fair loss valuations, early dispute resolution, and timely payments for valid claims, ultimately aiming to address rising insurance premiums and market instability.

bills.title: NY S08585 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill, known as the "climate accountability and loss recovery act," allows the New York Attorney General to sue "covered entities" for costs and losses stemming from "climate disasters." A "climate disaster" is defined as an event like a fire, flood, or storm where climate change was a significant factor in its occurrence or severity. A "covered entity" is a company with at least $500 million in market capitalization or annual revenue that is involved in the extraction, production, manufacture, or wholesale sale of "fossil fuel products" (coal, fossil fuel, fuel gases, and petroleum products) and has sufficient ties to New York State. The bill establishes that these covered entities are strictly liable, meaning they can be held responsible regardless of fault, for damages and losses related to climate disasters that result in a "market disruption," which is a significant negative impact on the availability, affordability, or stability of property and casualty insurance. This disruption can include insurers withdrawing from the market, increased premiums, or greater reliance on state-backed insurance programs. The Attorney General can seek recovery for assessments imposed on insurers, surcharges or premium increases passed on to policyholders due to climate disasters, and costs incurred by policyholders when these are transferred to residual market mechanisms. The bill also amends existing insurance law to ensure that any money recovered under this act is considered when making insurance rates.

bills.title: LA HB1187 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies how the Louisiana Citizens Property Insurance Corporation (Citizens), a state-created entity that provides property insurance, handles money from emergency assessments, which are special charges levied to cover unexpected deficits or claims, particularly after major disasters. It defines "excess emergency assessment monies" as funds from these assessments that are no longer needed to pay off bonds or other debts secured by that money, including associated fees and reserves. The bill allows Citizens to retain profits or excess reserves, previously limited to offsetting deficits, to also fund the Louisiana Fortify Homes Program, a state initiative aimed at strengthening homes against hurricanes. Crucially, any remaining "excess emergency assessment monies" can only be used for future debts or the Louisiana Fortify Homes Program after all existing bonds or debts related to these assessments have been fully paid off or legally accounted for. The bill also outlines procedures for certifying when these debts are settled, for transferring the excess funds, and allows Citizens to use a reasonable amount of these funds for administrative costs related to managing and transferring the money, requiring reporting to legislative committees and public posting of relevant resolutions and financial details.

bills.title: IA HF2766 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, effective July 1, 2026, makes several changes to Iowa's laws regarding captive insurance companies and introduces provisions for life captive reinsurance companies, including civil penalties. Key changes include making tax returns filed by captive and life captive reinsurance companies confidential and not subject to public inspection, with penalties for unauthorized disclosure, while allowing information sharing with federal and other state tax officials under specific agreements. The bill also clarifies that "captive company" and "captive reinsurance company" do not include "life captive reinsurance companies," and it expands the definition of "alien captive company" to include those formed in any country outside the United States. It also allows captive companies to accept or transfer risk via parametric contracts and introduces new definitions and regulations for life captive reinsurance companies, including requirements for their formation, operation, capital and surplus, reporting, and examinations, with specific provisions for their names, actuarial certifications, plans of operation, and investments, while also establishing rules for dormant life captive reinsurance companies and outlining conditions under which their certificates of authority can be suspended or revoked.

bills.title: MD HB1219 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill mandates that the University System of Maryland conduct a comprehensive study to examine the connections between climate change, the availability and cost of homeowner's insurance, and the state's readiness for emergencies and disasters. This study will be conducted in collaboration with various state agencies, including the Department of Natural Resources, Department of the Environment, Maryland Department of Emergency Management, and the Maryland Insurance Administration, as well as representatives from the Senate, House of Delegates, county and municipal emergency managers, and the Independent Insurance Agents of Maryland. The research will assess how climate change impacts insurance availability and pricing, analyze the effects of federal disaster preparedness policies and funding, evaluate how federal funds have bolstered emergency management capabilities as defined by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), and identify existing local resources for disaster recovery. By July 1, 2027, the University System of Maryland must present its findings and recommendations to the General Assembly. This report will include an overview of the National Flood Insurance Program and the private flood insurance market, and propose strategies for the state to encourage residents to invest in home protection against extreme weather through incentives like tax credits, waivers, grants, or loans. It will also suggest potential changes to insurance laws to better reward homeowners who have made resilience-enhancing improvements or live in areas with improved infrastructure, and explore best practices for funding emergency management, including dedicated funding mechanisms and allocation ratios for programs and disaster relief. Up to $100,000 from the Strategic Energy Investment Fund is authorized to support this study.

bills.title: LA HB904 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies existing laws concerning captive insurance companies, which are specialized insurance companies formed to insure the risks of their owners or members, and risk retention groups, which are similar entities formed by businesses with common liability exposures. Key provisions include allowing the commissioner to waive certain requirements for risk retention groups if it doesn't violate national insurance standards, and empowering the commissioner to create rules for captive insurers and risk retention groups to meet these national standards. The bill also expands the acceptable forms of capital and surplus for these companies, permits the commissioner to set different capital and surplus requirements based on the business type, and allows for increased deposit requirements for captive insurers and risk retention groups to ensure financial solvency. It clarifies that captive insurers and risk retention groups can be owned by other insurance companies, and establishes that risk retention groups must adhere to corporate governance and financial reporting standards set by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC), a body that develops uniform insurance regulations. Additionally, the bill allows the commissioner to use premium tax revenues collected from these entities to cover regulatory and enforcement expenses, and gives the commissioner discretion to require rate filings from captive insurers and risk retention groups, with the ability to disapprove filings that raise solvency concerns.

bills.title: CO SB049 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill expands eligibility for natural disaster mitigation assistance to include individual homeowners and homeowners' associations, in addition to government entities. It also clarifies that "natural disaster mitigation" includes installing "impact-resistant roofing materials," which are certified by Underwriters Laboratories as Class Four for hail resistance, and undertaking "property-specific mitigation actions," which are science-based actions like those recognized by the "Wildfire Prepared Home" designation. Furthermore, the bill creates a new income tax deduction for contributions made to a "catastrophe savings account" (CSA), a savings account designed to help homeowners cover insurance deductibles or uninsured losses related to hail, wildfire, or catastrophic wind events, as well as costs for property-specific mitigation. Interest earned on these CSAs will also be exempt from income tax, and these accounts are protected from legal seizure. This initiative aims to encourage homeowners to invest in measures that reduce their risk from natural disasters.

bills.title: CA SB1162 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires the Department of Forestry and Fire Protection, in consultation with the Department of Insurance and the California FAIR Plan Association, to make recommendations by June 30, 2027, to the Wildfire and Forest Resilience Task Force. The California FAIR Plan Association is an organization that helps provide basic property insurance to people who cannot get it through normal channels. The recommendations should focus on specific wildfire risk reduction measures in areas identified in the state's Wildfire and Forest Resilience Action Plan, particularly in high-hazard landscapes and communities, and especially in ZIP codes that have experienced the biggest increase in FAIR Plan policies sold since 2019. This aims to reduce wildfire hazards and improve the availability and affordability of homeowners' insurance in areas prone to wildfires, known as the wildland-urban interface.

bills.title: CA SB1182 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires that for any safety elements updated on or after January 1, 2028, local governments must include information on the availability of insurance for existing and planned developments within very high fire hazard severity zones and state responsibility areas, with the Office of Land Use and Climate Innovation tasked with updating guidance on where to find this information by July 1, 2027. Additionally, starting June 1, 2031, developers of new projects in high or very high fire hazard severity zones who cannot obtain at least two insurance quotes must ensure their structures meet specific wildfire building standards, with those in high fire zones needing to meet standards for very high fire zones, and those in very high fire zones needing to meet the highest wildfire building standards from the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety. Prior to seeking land use approvals for projects in these zones, developers must make a good-faith effort to get at least two insurance quotes estimating the cost to insure the proposed structures or units, and submit these quotes, or a report of their inability to obtain them, to the city or county, as well as to the Department of Real Estate and the Department of Insurance, and also disclose this information to potential buyers or renters. This bill applies to all cities, including charter cities, as it addresses a statewide concern.

bills.title: CA SB1404 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill reinstates a fire prevention fee on habitable structures within state responsibility areas, which are regions identified as having a high risk of wildfire. This fee, previously suspended, will be reinstated effective January 1, 2027, and will continue indefinitely, removing the previous January 1, 2031, repeal date. The funds collected are intended to support various fire prevention activities, including forest management, vegetation treatments, hazard mapping, evacuation planning, grants for local fire safe councils, community conservation corps projects, educational programs, and law enforcement related to arson investigations. The bill also repeals existing provisions related to the fire prevention fee program, consolidating the fee's reinstatement and indefinite continuation.

bills.title: CA SB1297 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a framework for regional wildfire partnerships to address wildfire risks by authorizing the formation of "regional wildfire partnerships," which can take the form of joint powers authorities, composed of various local government entities, special districts, and tribal governments. These partnerships are empowered to develop comprehensive wildfire mitigation plans aimed at reducing wildfire risk through projects like structure hardening and retrofitting, with the goal of improving insurance availability and affordability in their regions. The California Infrastructure and Economic Development Bank (I-Bank) is authorized to issue revenue bonds to finance these regional plans, with proceeds deposited into a new "Regional Wildfire Partnership Revolving Fund" that is continuously appropriated to support these partnerships. Partnerships receiving funds must enter into repayment and participation agreements with the I-Bank, which will also provide technical assistance. The bill also revises the existing Regional Forest and Fire Capacity Program to better support these regional efforts and requires partnerships to submit annual reports to the Department of Conservation, which will be made publicly available.

bills.title: FL H0883 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes and regulates "protected cell captive insurance companies," which are a type of insurance company that can segregate assets and liabilities into separate accounts called "protected cells" to insure the risks of specific participants. These protected cells are insulated from the general assets and liabilities of the main company, and their assets are only liable for the obligations of that specific cell. The bill revises definitions for "captive insurance company" and "special purpose captive insurance company," defines new terms like "participant," "participant contract," "protected cell," "protected cell assets," "protected cell captive insurance company," and "sponsor," and sets minimum capital and surplus requirements for these new entities. It also specifies that protected cell captive insurance companies can only insure the risks of their participants, requires them to file detailed operational plans and financial reports with the Office of Insurance Regulation (OIR), and outlines procedures for establishing and maintaining protected cells, including requirements for their names, assets, and liabilities. The bill further details how legal actions involving protected cell captive insurance companies and their cells should be handled, clarifies that assets of one cell cannot be used to pay for the obligations of another, and allows for the conversion, disaffiliation, merger, or combination of protected cells under specific conditions, all subject to OIR approval.

bills.title: MN SF5106 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes requirements for utilities to create and implement wildfire mitigation plans, which are detailed strategies for preventing and managing wildfires caused by utility infrastructure. These plans must outline how utilities will manage hazardous vegetation (plants near power lines that could cause fires), inspect and operate their equipment, coordinate with other utilities, and make necessary upgrades to reduce wildfire risk. For electric utilities, plans must also include procedures for restoring power after a wildfire, estimated costs, and public outreach efforts. The bill allows utilities to recover the costs of implementing these approved plans from their customers and limits their liability for wildfires if they can demonstrate substantial compliance with their mitigation plan, meaning they made good faith efforts to follow it, even if access issues or other factors prevented full execution. This legislation aims to proactively address wildfire risks associated with utility operations by requiring comprehensive planning and offering a framework for cost recovery and liability protection.

bills.title: NC S1055 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, the Carolina Housing Promise Act, aims to address housing affordability and accessibility in North Carolina by increasing the bonding authority of the Housing Finance Agency from $12 billion to $18 billion to finance housing projects, and by modifying the excise tax on real property conveyances to generate additional revenue for housing initiatives. It establishes a Housing Innovation Office to research and implement solutions for affordable, supportive, and sustainable housing, and allocates significant funding to the Housing Trust Fund for affordable housing development. The bill also introduces tax credits for donations to the Housing Trust Fund, allows for "by right" development of accessory dwelling units and higher-density housing near transit, and streamlines the approval process for affordable housing developments. Furthermore, it creates a first-time home buyer savings account with tax deductions, funds programs for rural water infrastructure, manufactured home repair, and the conversion of hotels and motels into affordable rental units, while also establishing an emergency housing response fund and a voluntary buyout fund for disaster-prone areas. The bill also includes provisions for tenant protections, such as emergency rental assistance, the right to counsel in eviction proceedings, and alternative security deposit options, and aims to prevent homelessness through combined housing and wraparound services. Finally, it invests in construction workforce development, promotes fair housing by investigating discriminatory practices, and supports infrastructure development in underserved communities, while also requiring comprehensive plans to include equitable distribution of affordable housing.

bills.title: RI H8219 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the "Rhode Island Insurance Market Protection Act," aims to regulate property insurers to align their practices with climate change mitigation goals, requiring "covered insurers" – those writing over $10 million in property and casualty insurance in Rhode Island and whose investments may be exposed to climate risk – to report on their investments and underwriting activities related to fossil fuels. Specifically, it prohibits underwriting and investing in new fossil fuel projects starting July 1, 2026, and mandates a phase-out of all underwriting and investments in existing fossil fuel projects and companies by January 1, 2035, with specific timelines and benchmarks. Insurers must also submit annual reports detailing their fossil fuel investments, financed emissions (emissions from investments), underwriting activities, insured emissions (emissions from underwriting), and strategies for complying with these science-based climate targets, which are defined as emissions reduction targets aligned with limiting global warming to well below 2°C. The bill also establishes a structured process for insurers wishing to withdraw from the state, requiring public notice, comment, and approval from the director of the department of business regulation (DBR) to ensure market stability and solvency. Failure to comply with these reporting, divestment, or withdrawal requirements can result in significant penalties, including administrative fines proportional to market share and profits, and restrictions on an insurer's ability to operate in Rhode Island, with escalating penalties for repeated violations. The DBR is tasked with developing the specific rules and processes for these requirements and will report biennially on the implementation and effects of the act, including its impact on insurance affordability and availability for underserved communities.

bills.title: NC S979 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill aims to increase transparency and fairness in insurance practices by modifying how insurers can charge rates higher than those officially approved by the state, a practice known as a "consent to rate deviation." For residential property insurance, insurers must now provide policyholders with at least 60 days' notice before charging a higher rate, detailing the difference between the approved rate and their proposed premium, and explaining the specific reasons for the higher charge, along with ways the policyholder can mitigate those reasons. The bill also significantly expands the data that insurance companies must report to the Department of Insurance (DOI), including detailed information on policy numbers, applications, non-renewals, claims, premiums, deductibles, and even demographic data of policyholders and the location of insured properties, all to be made publicly available in an online database after personally identifiable information is removed. Crucially, the bill prohibits insurers from using credit scoring as a basis for terminating or increasing rates on existing noncommercial private passenger motor vehicle and residential property insurance policies, though it still allows credit scoring for rate discounts. Furthermore, the DOI is directed to study how to gradually reduce the overall use of consent to rate deviations, aiming to have no more than 20% of the insurance market utilize them by 2040, and to conduct a public awareness campaign about these deviations, with $50,000 appropriated for these initiatives.

bills.title: HI SB1166 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill allows the Hawaii Property Insurance Association (HPPA), the Hawaii Hurricane Relief Fund (HHRF), and private insurers to sue "responsible parties" for costs incurred due to climate disasters and extreme weather events that are linked to climate change. A "responsible party" is defined as any entity that engaged in misleading practices about the connection between their fossil fuel products and climate change. The bill establishes a new legal cause of action for these insurers to recover "qualifying damages," which are defined as claim payments made to policyholders for losses from climate-related events. It also mandates that the HPPA must factor any money recovered from these lawsuits into its insurance rates. The bill specifies that these lawsuits can be filed within three years of discovering an injury and that responsible parties are jointly, severally, and strictly liable for damages. Importantly, the bill lists numerous defenses that responsible parties cannot use, such as ignorance of the law or reliance on previous court decisions. The bill also clarifies that it does not limit existing laws or remedies related to climate change and its impacts. The effective date of this bill is July 1, 3000.

bills.title: CT SB00453 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, effective October 1, 2026, will implement a five percent surcharge on property and casualty insurance policies issued or renewed in the state for fossil fuel infrastructure, such as wells, pipelines, terminals, refineries, and utility-scale generation facilities, that process, export, or transport oil, methane gas, or coal, excluding home fuel delivery vehicles. This surcharge will be collected by the Insurance Commissioner and deposited into a newly established "climate resilience account" managed by the Commissioner of Energy and Environmental Protection. The funds in this account, which will not lapse at the end of a fiscal year, are intended to be used for disseminating flood risk data to communities, raising public awareness about flood risks, and providing grants to communities for building or installing climate-resilient infrastructure to mitigate flooding.

bills.title: SC H4817 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the "Insurance Rate Reduction and Policyholder Protection Act," aims to enhance consumer protection and potentially lower insurance costs in South Carolina by enacting several key provisions. It expands the duties of the Director of the Department of Insurance to include recruiting insurance companies and making legislative recommendations for competitive markets, while also strengthening the Insurance Fraud Division's authority to investigate and prosecute insurance fraud, including new felony charges for staging motor vehicle collisions. The bill specifically targets deceptive practices by residential builders and contractors, prohibiting them from negotiating insurance claims on behalf of homeowners for roofing repairs or replacements and from offering to pay or rebate insurance deductibles as an incentive for business, with penalties for violations. It also introduces measures to encourage property loss mitigation and resilience, such as requiring insurers to offer discounts for fortified homes and expanding tax credits and creating a "Disaster Preparedness Sales Tax Holiday" for emergency supplies. Furthermore, the bill prohibits insurers from using uninsured and underinsured motorist claims as a basis for refusing to issue or renew automobile insurance policies or for setting premium rates, and mandates that insurers offer a zero-dollar deductible for automobile safety glass. Finally, it requires the Director of the Department of Insurance to report annually to the General Assembly on the impact of these changes on insurance rates and to provide testimony on the insurance market's status.

bills.title: OK HB2933 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill enacts several changes to Oklahoma insurance laws, primarily focusing on reporting requirements, penalties, and consumer protections. Insurers writing personal and commercial property insurance will be required to file annual reports with the Insurance Department detailing policy and claim data by ZIP code, which the Insurance Commissioner can use for market conduct examinations. Penalties for violations of insurance statutes and rules are increased, and the Commissioner gains the authority to refuse to renew, suspend, or revoke an insurer's license in addition to fines. The bill also clarifies that insurers cannot use traffic records older than three or five years (depending on the offense) to adjust motor vehicle insurance premiums or policies, and prohibits cancellations or non-renewals for certain traffic charges where the insured was acquitted, no charges were filed, or charges were dismissed. Furthermore, it introduces new protections for homeowners filing claims, including a "Homeowner Claims Bill of Rights" that insurers must provide, outlining policyholder rights and timelines for claim processing, and establishes a dispute resolution program with mediation services for certain insurance claims. The bill also modifies requirements for premium discounts related to fortified construction standards and introduces new provisions regarding roof age and inspections for homeowner's insurance policies, and clarifies that insurers cannot cancel or refuse to renew homeowner policies solely due to a first claim or inquiries, or for claims occurring more than five years prior. Finally, it adjusts timelines for claim acknowledgments, responses to inquiries, and claim investigations, and increases civil penalties for violations of unfair claims settlement practices.

bills.title: OK HB2955 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends the Oklahoma Captive Insurance Company Act to introduce new provisions and clarify existing ones, primarily focusing on the structure and operation of protected cells within sponsored captive insurance companies. Key changes include allowing the Insurance Commissioner to disclose information to the National Association of Insurance Commissioners under certain conditions, requiring captive insurance companies to respond to inquiries within a specified timeframe, and permitting irrevocable letters of credit from approved financial institutions to meet capital requirements. The bill also introduces new rules for mergers and acquisitions involving captive insurance companies, mandates administrative and accounting procedures for protected cells, and requires written approval for asset transfers related to protected cells. It clarifies that the creation of a protected cell does not create a separate legal entity and outlines procedures for the conversion of protected cells into other captive insurance structures or their merger with other protected cells. Furthermore, the bill establishes that protected cell assets are only available to creditors of that specific cell, not to general creditors of the sponsored captive insurance company, and clarifies how legal actions involving sponsored captive insurance companies and their protected cells should be handled, ensuring that unnamed protected cells are not parties to lawsuits and have no duty to defend other cells. The bill also addresses the management of assets and liabilities during rehabilitation or liquidation and allows public bodies to use public funds to capitalize captive insurance companies. Finally, it repeals an existing section related to conversions and mergers and sets an effective date of November 1, 2026.

bills.title: CA SB982 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill authorizes the Attorney General to file civil lawsuits against certain fossil fuel companies, referred to as "covered entities," for damages caused by climate disasters. These companies are defined as those with significant revenue or market capitalization that extract, produce, or sell fossil fuels and do business in California. The lawsuits can seek to recover costs and losses incurred by the California FAIR Plan Association (a state-backed insurance program for high-risk areas) and insurance policyholders who have faced increased premiums, loss of coverage, or higher costs due to climate disasters. Covered entities will be held strictly liable, meaning they are responsible regardless of fault, and courts can use principles like market share to determine their proportionate responsibility. The bill also prohibits these companies from passing on the costs of these lawsuits to California consumers through higher fuel prices, deeming such actions an unlawful business practice. Any money recovered, excluding restitution, will go into a new Attorney General Climate Disaster Fund for investigations, lawsuits, and enforcement related to climate disasters, with legislative appropriation for its use.

bills.title: CA SB1076 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, effective January 1, 2028, mandates that admitted insurers, meaning insurance companies licensed to do business in the state, cannot refuse to offer, sell, or renew residential property insurance for homes that meet established wildfire mitigation and building hardening standards, unless the refusal is based on underwriting guidelines unrelated to wildfire risk.  The bill also requires that any residential property insurance offered must provide coverage at least as broad as the insurer's most common policies.  Insurers can seek a temporary waiver from this requirement for specific geographic areas if they can demonstrate an "overconcentration of risk," meaning too many insured properties in one location could jeopardize the insurer's ability to pay claims, though such waivers do not permit refusal to renew existing policies.  Furthermore, the bill imposes a severe penalty: if an insurer habitually violates these provisions or chooses to stop offering residential property insurance rather than comply, their license to sell both residential property and automobile insurance in the state will be suspended or revoked for five years. The Insurance Commissioner is tasked with establishing the specific "home hardening and wildfire mitigation standards" and defining "overconcentration of risk" through regulations.

bills.title: LA HB880 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, known as the Louisiana Artificial Intelligence Insurance Fairness Act (LAIFA), aims to regulate the use of artificial intelligence (AI) and algorithmic decision systems (ADS) in the insurance industry to prevent unfair discrimination and ensure transparency. It establishes that AI and ADS, which are computational processes used by insurers for decisions like coverage, pricing, and claims, can perpetuate biases from historical data, disproportionately harming protected groups such as minority communities, lower-income individuals, and those in historically redlined areas. The Act mandates that insurers conduct annual audits to identify and address "disparate impact," which means statistically significant differences in insurance outcomes between protected classes and others, and prohibits the use of variables that act as "proxy variables" for protected class membership if they lead to discriminatory results, unless actuarially justified and no less discriminatory alternative exists. It also requires insurers to provide clear explanations to consumers when AI is used in adverse decisions, offers consumers a right to human review of AI-driven decisions, and establishes penalties for violations, including a private right of action for consumers who suffer harm. The bill also creates an AI Insurance Fairness Fund and an Advisory Board to oversee its implementation and ensure fair and responsible AI use in insurance.

bills.title: MA H1217 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Massachusetts Private Flood Insurance Act, which aims to create a regulatory framework for private flood insurance in the state. The legislation defines flood insurance types, including "standard" and "nonstandard" policies, and requires insurers to file rates and forms with the state's insurance commissioner using a "use and file" approach. The bill mandates that flood insurance policies must clearly indicate they are not National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) policies and must provide prominent disclosure of deductibles and policy limits. Insurers must establish rates based on actuarial data and cannot discriminate based on factors like age, race, or income. The act requires insurers to notify the commissioner before writing flood insurance, comply with existing insurance regulations, and provide customers with advance notice of policy renewals or cancellations. Surplus lines brokers are given flexibility to place flood insurance policies, and the Insurance Commissioner is tasked with tracking and annually reporting the number of private flood insurance policies in Massachusetts. The bill is designed to foster innovative flood insurance products, provide consumers with more choices, and create transparency in the private flood insurance market. The act will become effective 60 days after gubernatorial approval, with existing insurers having an additional 120 days to comply with the new requirements.

bills.title: HI SCR118 (2026)
bills.summary: This concurrent resolution urges the Insurance Division of the Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs to establish a working group to find ways to protect the state and its residents from the negative impacts of climate change on insurance, specifically its availability and affordability. The resolution highlights that climate disasters have caused significant harm, including property destruction and rising insurance costs, and threaten the stability of the insurance market, including the Hawaii Property Insurance Association (an insurer of last resort) and the state-funded Hawaii Hurricane Relief Fund. The working group is tasked with assessing insurance exposure to climate-related extreme weather events, identifying existing recovery mechanisms, and exploring legal options to seek financial recovery from responsible parties, such as major fossil fuel companies, who are alleged to have contributed to climate change through deceptive practices. The working group will include various state officials, including the Insurance Commissioner and Attorney General, as well as representatives from relevant boards and offices, and experts, and is expected to report its findings and recommendations, including any proposed legislation, by early 2027.

bills.title: NE LB611 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill updates Nebraska's building and energy codes by adopting the 2021 edition of several key international codes, replacing the previous 2018 editions. Specifically, the bill updates the state building code to incorporate the 2021 editions of the International Building Code (IBC) and International Residential Code (IRC), with some specific chapter and section modifications. The bill also updates the state's energy conservation code to the 2021 International Energy Conservation Code, which applies to all new buildings constructed with state funds. Local governments are required to adopt these updated codes within two years, and if they do not, the state building code will automatically apply. The legislation aims to improve energy efficiency, reduce energy consumption, lower import energy costs, and provide uniform standards for building construction across Nebraska. The bill includes provisions for local governments to make minor amendments to the codes for local conditions, but maintains a consistent baseline of building and energy standards statewide. The updates are designed to ensure minimum energy efficiency, comply with federal energy policies, and potentially reduce costs for consumers, especially low-income residents.

bills.title: HI SB17 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a Wildfire Mitigation Working Group, which will be administered by the Office of the State Fire Marshal and include representatives from various government agencies, experts, and stakeholders to develop strategies for reducing wildfire risk. The working group is tasked with creating a system of tax incentives and funding for land management, planning for retrofitting subdivisions with adequate evacuation routes, increasing workforce development in land management and wildfire mitigation, improving ignition-resistant building strategies, integrating food security and climate resilience with wildfire efforts, identifying funding for fuels management on private lands, considering vegetation management obligations for landowners and utilities, and integrating cultural stewardship and certified wildfire resilience standards. The Office of the State Fire Marshal will provide administrative support and prepare a report of the working group's findings and recommendations for the Legislature by 2028. The bill also appropriates funds for the working group's administrative expenses and revises previous appropriations made by Act 302, Session Laws of Hawaii 2025, to adjust the funded positions within the Office of the State Fire Marshal, specifically by removing funding for fire inspectors and fire investigators and adding funding for assistant fire marshals. This Act is set to take effect on July 1, 3000.

bills.title: NY S09942 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, known as the "Insure Our Communities Act," aims to address climate change impacts and insurance affordability, particularly for disadvantaged communities. It requires insurance companies to align their investments and underwriting practices with climate targets, prohibiting new investments in fossil fuel projects and mandating a phase-out of existing ones. The bill also introduces new reporting requirements for insurers, including data on their investments in fossil fuel companies and projects, as well as their underwriting activities related to these industries. Furthermore, it mandates that insurers assess their performance in meeting the insurance needs of their communities, considering factors like policyholder distribution, affordability of insurance, and financial support for climate resilience projects in low- and moderate-income and disadvantaged communities. The bill also prohibits refusing to issue or renew property and casualty insurance policies solely based on a property being located in a disadvantaged community, and it directs the Department of Financial Services to study methods for keeping property and casualty insurance affordable for these communities, including the potential for a public insurance option. Finally, it amends existing laws to include climate change risk management within the responsibilities of the Department of Financial Services and expands the definition of "banking institution" to include "covered insurance companies" for certain regulatory purposes.

bills.title: AL HB415 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the Department of Insurance; additional requirements for captive insurers specified, proposes several amendments to Alabama law concerning captive insurance companies, which are essentially insurance companies formed to insure the risks of their parent companies or affiliated groups. Key provisions include requiring captive insurers to maintain a financial account with an Alabama-approved financial institution, mandating the employment of a qualified captive manager and an independent certified public accountant and actuary, and requiring the submission of detailed operational plans, financial statements, and feasibility studies to the commissioner of insurance. The bill also increases the minimum capital and surplus requirements for various types of captive insurers, including pure captive insurers and protected cell captive insurers, and introduces new requirements for risk retention groups. Furthermore, it clarifies the formation processes for different captive structures and enhances the information that must be considered by the commissioner when approving their establishment, such as the competence of management and legal counsel, and the company's business plan. Finally, the bill mandates annual audited financial statements prepared using generally accepted accounting principles and an annual actuarial certification of loss reserves.

bills.title: AL SB308 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Office of Risk and Resilience within the Department of Insurance to develop and implement strategies for managing risks and promoting a stable insurance market in Alabama, and it places the Strengthen Alabama Homes Program under this new office. The Strengthen Alabama Homes Program, which aims to help homeowners make their properties more resistant to damage from hurricanes, tornadoes, and other windstorms, will now be administered by the Office of Risk and Resilience. The bill also makes minor technical changes to how the program applies for grants and adopts rules, and it specifies that the new office and the program's placement under it will become effective on June 1, 2026.

bills.title: MN SF5002 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a Hazard Mitigation Grant Program in Minnesota Statutes, chapter 12B, to help local governments prepare for and reduce the impact of emergencies.
