The program will create a dedicated fund, the hazard mitigation account, to provide grants and technical assistance to eligible applicants, which include counties, cities, school districts, and other local government entities. These grants can be used for "mitigation planning," which involves developing or updating plans to identify and address potential hazards, and for "mitigation projects," which are cost-effective actions designed to reduce risks to people and property from hazards, such as protecting critical infrastructure. The program aims to diminish the potential effects of emergencies through long-term risk reduction and can also serve as the local contribution for federal hazard mitigation programs like FEMA's BRIC (Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities) and HMGP (Hazard Mitigation Grant Program). The bill outlines eligible activities, the application process, and criteria for prioritizing grants, emphasizing cost-effectiveness, long-term risk reduction, and benefits to vulnerable communities. It also requires eligible applicants to contribute at least 25 percent of project costs, which can include donated materials or services, and mandates an annual report to the legislature on the program's awards and outcomes. Additionally, the bill appropriates $35 million for fiscal year 2027 for these grants, with a base appropriation of the same amount for fiscal years 2028 and 2029, and allows a portion of the funds for program administration and technical assistance.

bills.title: HI HB1949 (2026)
bills.summary:
This bill establishes the Green Fee Transparency and Accountability Program and Green Fee Resiliency Impact Dashboard, administered by the Hawaii Climate Change Mitigation and Adaptation Commission, and appropriates funds for fiscal years 2025-2026 and 2026-2027.

*   **Green Fee Resiliency Impact Dashboard Development and Maintenance:** $0 (amount not specified) from the General Fund is appropriated for fiscal year 2026-2027 to develop, implement, and maintain the dashboard. This funding is to be expended by the Hawaii Climate Change Mitigation and Adaptation Commission.
*   **Green Fee Projects Funding:** $0 (amount not specified) from the General Fund is appropriated for fiscal year 2025-2026 to be deposited into the Special Land and Development Fund. These funds will support green fee projects, with the total deposited not exceeding the green fee revenues collected and realized by the General Fund during that fiscal year. Any unexpended and unencumbered balances from this appropriation will lapse to the General Fund on June 30, 2028.
*   **Support for Various Programs:** $0 (amount not specified) from the Special Land and Development Fund is appropriated for fiscal year 2026-2027 to support a wide range of programs across various agencies. These programs are categorized under themes such as Climate/Hazard Resilience, Environmental Stewardship, and Sustainable Tourism, and include projects like irrigation public safety, invasive species removal, coral reef restoration, beach nourishment, cultural site protection, watershed management, and destination management. The funds are to be expended by the designated agencies for the purposes of the Act.

bills.title: OR HB4077 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends existing law to allow public utilities to include costs from their self-insurance or captive insurance programs, if approved by the Public Utility Commission, as "rate recovery expenditures." These expenditures, along with associated financing costs, can then be recovered from customers through "rate recovery bonds" and "rate recovery charges," which are essentially mechanisms to finance these costs and repay bondholders. The bill also clarifies the effective date of this change.

bills.title: HI SR111 (2026)
bills.summary: This resolution urges the Insurance Division of the Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs and the Department of the Attorney General to establish a working group to find ways to protect the state and its residents from the impacts of climate change on insurance. The working group will assess the state's exposure to climate-related disasters, identify existing recovery resources, and explore legal options to seek compensation from parties responsible for climate change, such as fossil fuel companies, to cover insurance costs. The group will include various state officials, including the Insurance Commissioner and Attorney General, as well as representatives from insurance associations and recovery offices, and will be tasked with sharing its findings and recommendations by early 2027.

bills.title: RI H7340 (2026)
bills.summary: This resolution respectfully urges Rhode Island insurers and the Rhode Island Property Insurance Association to lower insurance costs for local residents by taking legal action, known as subrogation claims, against companies that have knowingly misled the public about the link between their products and climate change. The resolution acknowledges that rising global temperatures, caused by increased carbon emissions, are leading to more severe weather events and other climate-related harms, which in turn have destabilized the insurance industry, leading to higher premiums and policy cancellations. It draws a parallel to past successful claims against industries like tobacco and those involved in the opioid epidemic, where financial burdens were shifted from policyholders and taxpayers to responsible parties. The resolution emphasizes Rhode Island's interest in protecting its citizens from climate disasters and ensuring affordable access to insurance, as well as safeguarding consumers from deceptive practices.

bills.title: HI HR137 (2026)
bills.summary: This resolution urges the Insurance Division of the Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs to establish a working group to find practical ways to shield the state and its residents from the negative impacts of climate change on insurance, specifically its availability and cost. The resolution highlights that climate disasters have already caused significant harm, including property destruction, financial losses, and increased insurance premiums, and that these disasters threaten the stability of the insurance market, including entities like the Hawaii Property Insurance Association (an "insurer of last resort," meaning it provides coverage when the private market won't) and the state-funded Hawaii Hurricane Relief Fund. The working group is tasked with assessing the state's insurance risks from climate-related extreme weather, identifying existing recovery resources, and exploring legal avenues, potentially involving the Attorney General, to seek financial recovery from responsible parties, such as major fossil fuel companies, who are seen as contributing to climate change through deceptive practices. The working group will include various state officials, legislative committee chairs, and experts, and is expected to report its findings and recommendations, including potential new laws, to legislative committees by early 2027, after which it will be dissolved.

bills.title: HI HCR145 (2026)
bills.summary: This concurrent resolution urges the Insurance Division of the Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs to establish a working group to explore ways to shield the state and its residents from the negative impacts of climate change on insurance, specifically its availability and cost. The resolution highlights that climate disasters have caused significant harm, including property destruction, financial losses, and increased insurance premiums, destabilizing the regular insurance market and "insurers of last resort" like the Hawaii Property Insurance Association, which are entities that provide insurance when the standard market fails. The working group is tasked with assessing the state's insurance risks from extreme weather, identifying existing recovery mechanisms, and investigating legal options, including potential lawsuits against responsible parties like major fossil fuel companies, to recover costs associated with these insurance exposures. The group will comprise various state officials, including the Insurance Commissioner and Attorney General, along with representatives from relevant boards and committees, and will be required to report its findings and recommendations, including any proposed legislation, by a specific date before the 2027 legislative session, after which it will be dissolved.

bills.title: CO HB1247 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill mandates that all homeowner's insurance policies issued or renewed in the state on or after January 1, 2027, must include a mandatory and binding appraisal process to resolve disputes over property damage claims, including the amount of loss, the cause of damage, and the necessary repairs. This appraisal process, which does not determine policy coverage, requires each party (the insured homeowner and the insurer) to select a qualified appraiser, and these appraisers will jointly select an umpire, who is either another appraiser or a retired judge, to help resolve disagreements. The decision of any two of these three individuals will determine the amount of loss. The bill outlines specific qualifications for appraisers and umpires to ensure they are fair and competent, meaning they have no financial interest in the outcome, are not current employees of either party, and have no personal relationships that could suggest bias. The insured and insurer will each pay for their own appraiser and split the cost of the umpire, with the entire appraisal process expected to be completed within four months of the umpire's selection. During this appraisal period, certain timelines within the insurance policy, such as those for recovering benefits or filing a lawsuit, will be paused (tolled). The bill also requires insurers to provide written evidence supporting their initial claim decisions and to reconsider claims if the insured submits third-party damage assessments. Finally, willfully or repeatedly violating these appraisal process requirements will be considered an unfair business practice.

bills.title: RI S2646 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the "Rhode Island Insurance Market Protection Act," aims to regulate property insurers to align their practices with climate change mitigation goals by limiting their involvement in fossil fuel projects and requiring greater transparency. Specifically, it defines "covered insurers" as those with significant property and casualty premiums in Rhode Island or whose investments pose climate-related risks, and it mandates that these insurers phase out underwriting and investments in new and existing fossil fuel projects by specific deadlines, aligning with "science-based climate mitigation targets" which are emissions reduction goals necessary to limit global warming. The bill also requires covered insurers to report their investments and underwriting related to fossil fuels, their "financed emissions" (emissions from investments) and "insured emissions" (emissions from underwriting), and their strategies for complying with these new requirements, with penalties for non-compliance including financial penalties proportional to market share and profit, and potential restrictions on their ability to operate in Rhode Island. Furthermore, it establishes a structured process for insurers wishing to withdraw from the state, requiring public notice, comment, and approval from the director of the department of business regulation (DBR) to ensure market stability and protect policyholders. The DBR is tasked with developing rules and processes to implement these requirements, and will report annually on the state of insurer climate risk readiness and compliance.

bills.title: RI S2769 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes minimum time limits for filing structural property damage claims, meaning insurance policies cannot shorten these periods. For claims involving fire or lightning damage, the minimum time to file is twenty-four months from when the damage was discovered or should have been discovered. For all other structural property damage, such as from other perils or causes, the minimum time limit is ten years from discovery, unless a shorter period is specifically allowed by law. The bill also prohibits insurers from claiming a claim is fully settled or closed with just a payment; a formal written release signed by the claimant or insured, witnessed, and notarized is required. Insurers must also clearly inform policyholders about their rights and the applicable time limits for filing claims. This legislation aims to protect consumers by ensuring they have adequate time to pursue claims and understand their rights, and it takes effect immediately upon passage.

bills.title: MA H1004 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill proposes significant updates to Massachusetts building codes and regulations to enhance climate resilience and safety. It establishes new definitions related to climate risks, including expanded flood maps and climate resilience, and modifies the state board of building regulations and standards to include experts in energy efficiency, advanced building technology, and climate adaptation. The bill creates a new "specialized stretch resilience code" that municipalities can opt into, which includes more stringent building requirements addressing future climate challenges such as flooding, extreme temperatures, and wind risks. The code will provide specific guidelines for building elevation, utility placement, structural integrity, and emergency preparedness, with requirements developed by an advisory committee of 17 experts from various relevant fields. Additionally, the bill mandates the creation of a statewide climate resilience retrofit program that will offer technical and financial assistance to help existing buildings meet new climate resilience standards, with a particular focus on environmental justice communities and areas at high risk of flooding. The overall goal is to proactively adapt building standards to anticipated climate change impacts, improving infrastructure safety and reducing potential damage from extreme weather events.

bills.title: PA HB1718 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends Pennsylvania law to require insurers and surplus lines licensees to inform individuals purchasing or renewing homeowner's insurance policies about flood insurance. Specifically, insurers must provide written notice about the availability and cost of flood insurance, and the Insurance Department will create a model disclosure form to guide this process, explaining that standard homeowner's policies typically don't cover flood damage and highlighting the benefits of separate flood insurance, including potential waiting periods and higher claim payments compared to disaster assistance. Surplus lines licensees must also inform prospective insureds in writing whether their policy covers flood damage. The bill defines key terms like "flood insurance policy" and "natural flood event," which refers to general inundation from various water sources or mudflows, and establishes that the Insurance Department will enforce these provisions and can impose penalties of up to $25,000 per calendar year for violations.

bills.title: IL HB2559 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends the Residential Real Property Disclosure Act to require sellers to disclose the age of the roof when selling a residential property. Specifically, the bill adds a new line item (number 25) to the existing disclosure report form, which asks sellers to indicate how many years old the roof of the property is. This change means that when a seller prepares a Residential Real Property Disclosure Report, they must now provide information about the roof's age, similar to how they currently disclose other potential material defects or issues with the property. The purpose of this requirement is to give prospective buyers more transparent information about the condition of the property, helping them make more informed decisions about a potential purchase. The disclosure is meant to reflect the current condition of the property and does not necessarily imply that an older roof is a problem, but rather provides buyers with additional context about the property's infrastructure.

bills.title: IL HB3594 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill creates the Extreme Weather Recovery Act, which establishes a legal mechanism for individuals, businesses, and associations to sue fossil fuel companies for damages caused by climate change and extreme weather events. The bill covers the period from 1965 to the present and allows harmed parties to bring civil actions against "responsible parties" (defined as fossil fuel companies that have emitted at least one billion metric tons of carbon dioxide) if they have suffered at least $10,000 in damages. The legislation is rooted in extensive legislative findings that detail how fossil fuel companies knew about climate change risks as early as the 1950s but continued to spread misinformation and prioritize profits. To bring a lawsuit, plaintiffs must prove that climate disasters or extreme weather events were substantially worsened or caused by the responsible party's products, with the bill providing broad definitions of what constitutes a climate disaster. The act allows for strict liability and permits plaintiffs to seek full compensatory and punitive damages, and it includes provisions that limit potential defenses companies might raise. Notably, the bill prohibits state and local government entities from bringing actions under the act, leaving it to individual plaintiffs to seek justice. The legislation aims to create a judicial forum for individuals to recover damages related to climate change impacts, emphasizing the state's compelling interest in protecting its citizens from climate-related harm.

bills.title: IL HB5529 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Strengthen Illinois Homes Program to help homeowners mitigate damage from catastrophic wind and hail events by creating a dedicated fund and setting up grant requirements for eligible entities like local governments and non-profits. The program, which aims to be operational by July 1, 2028, will provide grants of up to $15,000 to assist with upgrading homes to meet specific "mitigation program standards," such as those set by the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety (IBHS), and will prioritize lower-income households. Additionally, the bill introduces a Climate Risk Disclosure Law, requiring certain insurance companies to participate in climate surveys issued by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) to improve transparency on how they manage climate-related risks. It also mandates that insurance companies offer actuarially justified premium discounts for homeowners who fortify their properties against wind and hail damage and requires them to offer an optional rider or endorsement that covers the cost of upgrading a non-fortified home to meet these standards in the event of a qualifying claim, such as a total roof replacement. Finally, the bill includes provisions for insurance companies to provide clearer notices regarding flood coverage options and updates existing laws related to dental service plans, health maintenance organizations, and limited health service organizations to incorporate these new climate and home strengthening requirements.

bills.title: KS HB2128 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill authorizes the Kansas Insurance Commissioner to select and announce the version of certain insurance-related instructions, calculations, and documents that will be in effect for the upcoming calendar year, with the announcement to be published in the Kansas Register by December 1st each year. The bill updates several sections of Kansas insurance law, focusing on three main areas: (1) establishing procedures for the commissioner to specify which National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) instructions will be used for risk-based capital calculations and other financial reporting, (2) expanding exemptions for certain types of insurance entities and self-funded health plans, and (3) introducing new provisions related to group capital calculations and liquidity stress testing for insurance holding companies. The bill adds requirements for insurers to file group capital calculations and liquidity stress test results, while also establishing strict confidentiality rules around these financial documents. Additionally, the bill updates definitions related to health benefit plans and insurance entities, clarifying the scope of various insurance regulations. These changes aim to modernize Kansas insurance regulations, align them more closely with national standards, and provide the Insurance Commissioner with more flexible tools for overseeing insurance companies and holding systems.

bills.title: WA HB2089 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, known as the wildfire alleviation support act, aims to restore funding to Washington state's wildfire response, forest restoration, and community resilience account by modifying how certain financial institutions are taxed on loan interest. Specifically, it changes the definition of a "community bank" that was previously eligible for a tax break on interest earned from loans, particularly those secured by residential properties. Previously, a "community bank" was defined as a person or affiliate operating in ten or fewer states, intended to encourage lending in the residential housing market. However, a review found that a significant portion of the tax benefit went to large, out-of-state financial institutions, not the intended local lenders, and that these institutions' operations, like data centers, can contribute to climate warming and thus increase wildfire risk. This bill redefines the eligible entities to include "high volume mortgage lenders," which are either specified financial institutions or those with at least $10 billion in annual closed mortgage origination volume. The revenue generated from taxing interest from these larger lenders will be transferred from the state's general fund to the wildfire response account, starting in November 2027, to help fund wildfire preparedness and mitigation efforts.

bills.title: CT HB05317 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, effective July 1, 2026, amends existing law concerning flood damage and insurance notices for mortgage loans. It specifically defines a "mortgage loan" for the purposes of these notice requirements as a loan secured by a first mortgage on a one-to-four-family residential property in the state, made to finance the purchase of that property. The bill clarifies that creditors, which are entities making mortgage loans, must provide a written notice to applicants at least ten days before the closing of a mortgage loan transaction. This notice must inform the applicant that standard homeowners insurance does not cover flood damage, that flood damage can occur even outside designated flood zones, and that they should consider speaking with an insurance professional about flood insurance. The notice must be written in plain language, signed and dated by the applicant to confirm receipt, and a copy must be kept with the mortgage records.

bills.title: MN HF4581 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a Hazard Mitigation Grant Program in Minnesota, creating a dedicated fund to support local governments and other political subdivisions (referred to as "eligible applicants") in developing and implementing plans and projects that reduce the impact of emergencies. The program will provide grants for "mitigation planning," which involves activities like creating or updating hazard mitigation plans and conducting risk assessments, and for "mitigation projects," which are cost-effective initiatives designed to lessen risks to people and property from hazards. The fund can also be used to cover the non-federal portion of costs for federal hazard mitigation programs, such as FEMA's BRIC and HMGP programs. Eligible applicants must contribute at least 25 percent of project costs, with in-kind donations of materials, services, and labor counting towards this share. The commissioner will prioritize applications based on factors like cost-effectiveness, long-term risk reduction, and the extent to which projects address recurring disaster impacts, with specific consideration for flood-related projects and those benefiting vulnerable communities. The bill also mandates that the commissioner provide technical assistance to applicants and submit an annual report on grant awards and outcomes. Finally, it appropriates $35 million for fiscal year 2027 for these grants, with a base appropriation of $35 million for fiscal years 2028 and 2029, and allows a portion of the funds for program administration and technical assistance.

bills.title: HI HB2618 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a new "Green Fee Special Fund" to receive a portion of the revenue generated by the "green fee," which is a climate impact fee designed to protect Hawaii's natural resources and mitigate climate change effects. It also creates several other special funds, including ones for watershed protection, biodiversity, fire resilience, aquatic resource conservation, cesspool conversion loans, and coastal restoration, all of which will also receive allocations of green fee revenues. The bill mandates that the Governor must request funding for specific climate change and tourism destination management projects through a separate bill, not part of the main budget, for an amount that approximates the green fee revenues remaining after allocations to these various funds.

bills.title: MA H938 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a Climate & Community Resilience Fund to support climate change adaptation and mitigation, with a specific focus on low-income, moderate-income, and environmental justice populations. The fund will be administered by the Secretary of Energy and Environmental Affairs and supported by a Climate and Community Resilience Advisory Board composed of seven diverse members with expertise in areas such as environmental justice, housing, workforce development, and climate resilience. The fund will be primarily financed through a 2% property insurance fee, with potential incremental increases up to 5%, and will include revenues from various sources like grants, donations, and appropriations. Funds can be used for a wide range of purposes, including supporting community-based resilience centers, workforce development, municipal coordination, climate adaptation projects, and initiatives that prioritize marginalized communities. The bill explicitly prohibits using funds for carbon capture technologies or carbon market offsets, and emphasizes equitable distribution of resources, community participation, and transparent, flexible funding mechanisms. An annual report will be required detailing the fund's activities, expenditures, and progress toward meeting its goals, with a strong emphasis on benefiting historically underserved communities and addressing environmental inequities.

bills.title: FL S1028 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the operations of the Citizens Property Insurance Corporation by establishing separate clearinghouses for personal lines insurance, authorized commercial insurance, and surplus lines commercial insurance, with the goal of directing more policyholders to the private market. Specifically, it prohibits Citizens from issuing new coverage for commercial residential and nonresidential risks if a surplus lines insurer offers comparable coverage (meaning equivalent or better terms) and the total cost of that coverage is no more than 15% greater than Citizens' cost. The bill defines "total cost of insurance coverage" as the total annual premium plus all mandatory fees, taxes, and surcharges. It also mandates that Citizens establish these clearinghouses by January 1, 2027, and requires that certain risks be submitted to the commercial lines clearinghouse for surplus lines insurance under specific conditions. The bill also clarifies the roles and responsibilities of various parties involved, including insurers, agents, and clearinghouse administrators, and outlines procedures for sharing risk information and determining eligibility for coverage. Importantly, it requires the Office of Insurance Regulation to review and approve the program's operational standards and contracts.

bills.title: AL HB163 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the Property Insurance and Capital Expenditure Act, amends existing Alabama law to allow local governments to establish programs that facilitate private financing for property improvements aimed at reducing insurance costs and enhancing resilience. Key provisions include defining terms like "assessment agreement" (an agreement where a property owner allows a local government to place a lien on their property to secure repayment of financing), "capital provider" (a private entity providing the financing), and "consent agreement" (an agreement with mortgage holders that subordinates their liens to the new assessment lien). The bill clarifies that "qualified projects" can include improvements for wind resistance, safe rooms, battery storage, flood mitigation, and energy/water efficiency, and that these programs apply to commercial, industrial, agricultural, and multi-family properties (excluding smaller residential buildings). Local governments can designate regions for these programs, and while they facilitate the process, the financing itself comes from private "capital providers." The bill explicitly states that public funds cannot be used to fund or repay these private loans, and local government officials are protected from liability for decisions made under the program. It also repeals provisions related to a loss reserve fund and state regulatory jurisdiction, and establishes that the billing, collection, and enforcement of these assessment liens are the responsibility of the capital provider, not the local government.

bills.title: AL HB424 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a new Office of Risk and Resilience within the Department of Insurance to develop and implement strategies for managing risks and promoting a stable insurance market in Alabama, with the Commissioner of Insurance authorized to hire staff, including one exempt employee. Additionally, the bill moves the Strengthen Alabama Homes Program, which provides grants to retrofit insurable properties against windstorm damage, under the purview of this new office, and clarifies that the program can also fund nonprofit entities for similar retrofitting projects, with all mitigation efforts requiring local permits and inspections, and the department empowered to adopt necessary rules and eligibility requirements for grant administration.

bills.title: FL S0234 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires insurers to provide detailed annual financial information to the Office of Insurance Regulation about transactions with their affiliates and managing general agents (MGAs), with the goal of ensuring transparency and preventing financial manipulation. Specifically, insurers must submit comprehensive reports detailing the costs and descriptions of services provided by affiliates, including benchmarking studies that compare fees to market rates, and certify that their affiliate fees do not exceed 20% of gross written premiums (or explain why they do if they exceed that threshold). The bill mandates that the Office of Insurance Regulation contract with an independent third-party entity annually to review these transactions and identify any that are not fair and reasonable. Additionally, the bill prohibits insurers from engaging in transactions designed to misrepresent their financial condition, such as forgiving fees or creating circular transactions, and prevents insurers in hazardous financial condition from paying dividends or issuing executive bonuses. Insurers must also publicly post this financial information on their websites, which will no longer be considered trade secret and will be subject to public disclosure. The bill aims to increase accountability and transparency in insurers' financial operations and protect against potential financial misconduct.

bills.title: FL S0230 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill enhances transparency in the insurance industry by establishing new requirements for trade secret claims and financial disclosures. It defines a "trade secret" according to existing state law and requires detailed documentation when companies claim trade secret protection for submitted information. The bill specifically identifies several categories of information that cannot be considered trade secrets, including financial data used in rate calculations, transactions between insurers and affiliates, employee compensation, dividends, rate filings, and information related to proposed legislation. The Office of Insurance Regulation must review all trade secret claims and deny those that do not meet legal standards, with the requirement to issue a written notice of denial. The bill mandates that the Financial Services Commission contract with an independent third-party entity every two years to review trade secret claims and report findings to legislative leadership. Companies that knowingly make false trade secret claims can face administrative fines up to $25,000 and potential suspension or revocation of their insurance license. Additionally, the bill requires all fees, commissions, and profit-sharing agreements between insurers and their affiliates to be filed with the office and made publicly accessible on the department's website, with the provisions set to take effect on July 1, 2026.

bills.title: FL H0275 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the rate-setting rules for Citizens Property Insurance Corporation, Florida's state-backed insurance provider of last resort. Currently, the corporation is limited to annual rate increases of 12% in 2023, rising to 15% by 2026, for policies covering primary residences. The bill introduces a significant change by removing these rate increase limitations for new policies issued on or after June 1, 2026, and their subsequent renewals. However, this exemption does not apply to new policies in counties where the state determines there is not a reasonable level of insurance market competition. The bill maintains the existing requirement that Citizens' rates must be actuarially sound and not competitive with private market rates, ensuring it remains a backstop for insurance coverage. The goal appears to be giving Citizens more flexibility in pricing new policies while still protecting homeowners in areas with limited insurance options. The change could potentially allow Citizens to set rates closer to true risk levels, which might impact insurance costs for Florida homeowners.

bills.title: FL S0634 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends Florida's insurance regulations for Citizens Property Insurance Corporation, a state-run insurer of last resort, by modifying rate increase limitations. Specifically, for any order establishing rates on or after June 1, 2026, the current restrictions on annual rate increases (which cap increases at 12% in 2023, rising to 15% by 2026) will no longer apply to new policies or their subsequent renewals. The bill includes an important exception: this deregulation does not apply to new policies covering primary residences in counties where the state determines there is not a reasonable level of insurance market competition. The legislation aims to provide more flexibility in pricing for Citizens Property Insurance Corporation, potentially allowing for more actuarially sound and market-responsive rates for certain types of policies. The bill maintains the corporation's core purpose as a residual market mechanism to provide insurance when coverage cannot be obtained in the voluntary market, while giving the insurer more latitude in setting rates for non-primary residence policies after June 2026.

bills.title: FL S0832 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill introduces several key provisions related to residential property insurance in Florida, focusing on increasing transparency and consumer protection. Beginning October 1, 2026, property insurers will be required to submit rate transparency reports with their rate change filings, which must include detailed breakdowns of rating factors such as reinsurance costs, claims costs, defense expenses, fees and commissions, and profit percentages. Insurers must provide these reports to consumers along with policy offers and renewals, clearly indicating if the report is preliminary and subject to modification. The Office of Insurance Regulation will be mandated to establish a comprehensive website resource center with detailed information about insurance markets, coverage options, claim processes, and consumer rights. Additionally, the bill prohibits insurers from including land value when establishing coverage amounts or adjusting claims for dwellings, and updates the Homeowner Claims Bill of Rights with minor technical changes. The law aims to help consumers better understand insurance rates, claim processes, and their rights by requiring more straightforward, graphical, and accessible information from insurers. The provisions will take effect on July 1, 2026, giving insurers and regulators time to prepare for the new requirements.

bills.title: FL H0767 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill aims to enhance transparency and consumer protections in residential property insurance. It mandates the Office of Insurance Regulation (OIR) to create and maintain a comprehensive online resource center with plain-language explanations of insurance market dynamics, company financial information, available insurance options, and details on mitigation credits and programs like the My Safe Florida Home Program. This information will not be considered a trade secret and will be publicly accessible. Insurers will be required to notify policyholders of this resource center with every offer of coverage and policy renewal. Additionally, insurers will be prohibited from including the value of land when determining coverage amounts or adjusting claims for homes, unless the property is on the shoreline or surrounded by water. The bill also requires that notices about premium discounts for hurricane loss mitigation include information on whether the insurer offers enhanced discounts for roof systems with secondary water resistance. Finally, it updates a cross-reference within the Homeowner Claims Bill of Rights to reflect changes in other sections of the law.

bills.title: FL H0909 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the rules for Citizens Property Insurance Corporation, Florida's state-backed insurance provider of last resort, by updating eligibility requirements, rate increase provisions, and flood insurance mandates. The legislation increases the maximum dwelling replacement cost threshold for properties eligible for Citizens coverage in counties with limited insurance competition, from $1 million to $1.5 million. It also establishes a structured annual rate increase framework, allowing Citizens to incrementally raise rates by 12% in 2023, escalating to 15% by 2026 for most policies, with a lower 10% cap in counties with limited insurance competition. The bill also adjusts flood insurance requirements, phasing in mandatory flood coverage for residential properties based on dwelling replacement cost and location, with specific exemptions for certain policy types like wind-only or condominium unit policies, and properties in low-risk flood zones or with elevated structures. The underlying intent remains to provide affordable property insurance in areas where private insurers are reluctant to offer coverage, while gradually aligning Citizens' rates and risk management practices with market conditions. The changes aim to make Citizens more financially sustainable and reduce its role as a primary insurance provider by encouraging private market participation.

bills.title: FL S1024 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the rules for Citizens Property Insurance Corporation, a state-run insurance entity designed to provide property insurance in Florida when private insurers are unwilling to do so. The bill changes eligibility requirements for property coverage, specifically adjusting the maximum dwelling replacement cost from $1 million to $1.5 million in counties with limited insurance competition. It mandates annual rate increases for Citizens policies, capping these increases at 12% in 2023, rising to 15% by 2026. The bill also updates flood insurance requirements, phasing in mandatory flood coverage for properties with higher replacement costs between 2024 and 2027, while creating exceptions for certain policy types like condominium unit owner policies and policies in specific flood zones. Additionally, the bill provides more flexibility for properties in areas with limited insurance competition, allowing some properties to be exempt from flood insurance requirements. These changes aim to balance affordable insurance access with financial sustainability for the state-run insurance corporation, addressing challenges in Florida's property insurance market.

bills.title: FL H1399 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes new regulations for property insurance companies and their "affiliates," which are defined as entities controlled by or controlling the insurer through ownership, common management, or shared management. Property insurers must now provide documentation to the Office of Insurance Regulation (OIR) demonstrating that financial considerations and payments made to affiliates are fair and reasonable, considering factors like the affiliate's costs, the financial health of both entities, and whether contracts benefit policyholders. Insurers must also submit audited financial statements annually for review by the OIR, and contracts with affiliates executed after July 1, 2026, will require a termination clause and office approval for any extensions. During declared states of emergency, the OIR can restrict fund transfers from insurers to affiliates for up to 60 days, and violations can result in penalties, including fines and license suspension or revocation, with affiliates potentially being required to refund unfair payments. Furthermore, affiliates will need to register with the OIR starting January 1, 2027, providing detailed information about their operations and personnel, and their registrations must be renewed annually, with administrative and criminal penalties for non-compliance. The bill also amends existing law to include the profits, revenues, and investment income of affiliates as a factor the OIR must consider when determining if property insurance rates are excessive.

bills.title: FL S1726 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the "Real Affordable Housing Relief Act," makes several changes to Florida law aimed at increasing affordable housing and improving home resiliency. It removes prohibitions on local governments enacting rent control measures and establishes Community Land Bank Programs, allowing counties and municipalities to acquire, hold, and transfer unimproved real property for affordable housing development. The bill also introduces a uniform home resiliency grading scale to measure a home's ability to withstand severe weather, creates a pilot program for innovative mitigation solutions in housing, and establishes tax credits for redeveloping shopping centers into workforce housing and for affordable housing construction loans. Additionally, it strengthens antitrust laws by prohibiting certain business entities from acquiring large numbers of single-family homes for rental purposes and restricts the use of certain pricing coordination services by landlords. The bill also grants the consumer advocate more power to challenge insurance rate filings and requires specific reporting for certain housing trust funds.

bills.title: FL H1493 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the "Real Affordable Housing Relief Act," makes several significant changes to Florida law aimed at increasing housing affordability and resilience. It removes prohibitions on local governments enacting rent control measures and establishes Community Land Bank Programs, allowing municipalities and counties to acquire and manage real property for affordable housing development. The bill also introduces a uniform home resiliency grading scale to assess a home's ability to withstand severe weather, a pilot program for innovative mitigation solutions, and tax credits for redeveloping shopping centers into workforce housing. Additionally, it creates an Affordable Housing Construction Loan Program and strengthens antitrust laws by prohibiting certain business entities from acquiring large numbers of single-family homes for rental purposes, aiming to prevent corporate landlords from dominating the single-family housing market. The bill also revises the powers of the consumer advocate in insurance rate filings and mandates reporting on the use of certain housing trust fund monies.

bills.title: FL S1716 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill removes provisions that allowed Citizens Property Insurance Corporation, a state-backed insurer of last resort, to resolve disputes about its claim determinations through proceedings before the Division of Administrative Hearings (DOAH), which is an administrative body that handles certain legal disputes involving state agencies. Previously, Citizens could adopt policy forms that directed these disputes to DOAH, and these proceedings had specific rules regarding attorney fees and offers of settlement, similar to court cases. The bill effectively eliminates this specific avenue for dispute resolution with Citizens, meaning disputes will likely be handled through other existing legal or administrative channels.

bills.title: WA SB5928 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires insurers in Washington State to provide greater transparency regarding wildfire risk scores used in property insurance. If an insurer uses a wildfire risk score to determine eligibility for coverage, set premiums, or both, and this score leads to a nonrenewal, cancellation, or an increase in premiums, the insurer must disclose to the policyholder and their insurance producer the current wildfire risk score, the possible range of scores, the entity that created the score, its creation date, and the key factors that negatively impacted the score, along with steps consumers can take to improve it if the factors are within their control. For premium increases, this information must be included in the renewal notice, and for nonrenewals or cancellations, it must be in the respective notices. Insurers must also allow policyholders to request a revised wildfire risk score or classification once per policy period if they provide documentation of property-specific or community-level mitigation actions that reduce wildfire risk. Policyholders can appeal their wildfire risk score or related discounts directly to the insurer, who must acknowledge and respond to the appeal within specific timeframes. Furthermore, insurers must file their wildfire risk models, including descriptions, impact on rates, actuarial justifications, and how they account for mitigation activities, with the Insurance Commissioner, with this information generally kept confidential as trade secrets. The models must also incorporate property-specific and community-level mitigation actions, and insurers must post information on their websites about available discounts for mitigation efforts and the appeal process.

bills.title: WA SB6079 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, titled the "Strengthen Washington Homes Act," aims to reduce non-renewal and cancellations of insurance policies due to wildfire risk by establishing a grant program and modifying existing insurance regulations. The core of the bill is the creation of the "Strengthen Washington Homes Program," which will provide financial grants to property owners, contractors, non-profits, and tribes to help mitigate wildfire risks for insurable dwellings, defined as any residential structure. These mitigation efforts must align with wildfire preparedness standards set by the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety (IIBHS) or a similar entity. The bill also amends existing law to ensure that insurers cannot use wildfire risk as a sole reason to deny property insurance if the property meets certain wildfire preparedness designations, though other risk factors can still be considered. A dedicated "Strengthen Washington Homes Program Account" will be established to fund these grants, with provisions for accepting donations and legislative appropriations. The bill also includes language about pilot projects to inform the program's implementation and allows the commissioner to adopt rules for administering the program, including eligibility criteria and grant distribution procedures. Additionally, it modifies how certain state funds are managed, specifically allowing unexpended funds from the insurance commissioner's regulatory and fraud accounts to be used to fund the new program account until a specific date.

bills.title: MA S2919 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a special commission to investigate the rising costs and availability of homeowners insurance for residents of manufactured and mobile homes in Massachusetts. The commission, composed of 15 members including legislative leaders, state officials, representatives from insurance and manufactured housing associations, a municipal official, and consumer advocates, will examine the reasons behind increasing premiums, the current insurance market's participation and concentration, the impact of state regulations, the adequacy of existing consumer protections like the FAIR Plan, and how Massachusetts compares to other states. The commission's goal is to identify potential policy and legislative solutions to improve coverage options, affordability, and long-term market stability for these homeowners, and it must submit its findings and recommendations within twelve months of the bill's passage.

bills.title: NH HB1056 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a legislative commission to comprehensively study the impacts of reinsurance on property insurance costs and availability in New Hampshire, with a specific focus on how extreme weather events affect insurance rates. The commission will consist of six members, including four representatives from the state legislature, two senators, the insurance commissioner, and two insurance industry representatives (one from a large carrier and one from a small local carrier). The commission's specific research objectives include examining how global extreme weather events influence reinsurance rates, understanding how future weather events are considered in rate-setting, developing a process for transparency in reinsurance costs, and evaluating the availability of reinsurance for small local carriers and potential economic consequences of reinsurance market disruptions. Reinsurance, which is a risk management tool where insurance companies purchase additional insurance to protect themselves from large-scale losses, has been contributing to rising property insurance premiums, with rates increasing approximately 20 percent recently. The commission is required to submit a comprehensive report with findings and recommendations by November 1, 2026, and the bill includes provisions for legislative members to receive mileage compensation for their participation. The legislation aims to provide deeper insights into the complex dynamics affecting property insurance costs in New Hampshire.

bills.title: NH HB1582 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill prohibits insurance companies from using credit information, such as credit history or credit scores, when underwriting, rating, renewing, canceling, or deciding whether to issue personal automobile and homeowners insurance policies. The bill also restricts insurers' surveillance practices by prohibiting the use of drones to inspect private property without the explicit written permission of the property owner, and banning the use of satellite imagery or commercial observation tools to increase premiums, cancel, or decline to renew homeowners insurance policies. The legislation aims to protect consumers from potential unfair discrimination and privacy infringements, defining key terms like "consumer report" and establishing that any violations will be considered unfair and deceptive insurance practices. Insurance companies are specifically barred from coercing property owners into granting surveillance permissions, and the bill allows for such permissions to be voluntary, revocable, and limited in scope. The new regulations will take effect 180 days after the bill's passage, and while the fiscal impact is considered indeterminable, the Insurance Department anticipates potential challenges for insurers in adapting to these unique restrictions.

bills.title: AL SB207 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill, concerning captive insurance companies in Alabama, primarily aims to increase the minimum paid-in capital and surplus requirements for various types of captive insurers and introduces new reporting and operational requirements. Specifically, it raises the minimum capital for pure captive insurance companies and reinsurance captive insurance companies from $100,000 and $10,000 respectively to $250,000, and establishes a new minimum of $1,000,000 for risk retention groups. The bill also mandates that captive insurers employ or contract with a qualified captive manager, a certified public accountant, and actuaries, and requires them to submit detailed operational plans and feasibility studies to the commissioner. Furthermore, it introduces new annual reporting requirements, including an audited financial statement prepared in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) and an actuarial certification of loss reserves, with specific effective dates for these changes.

bills.title: CO HB1091 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes comprehensive data privacy protections for consumers regarding homeowner's insurance transactions, requiring insurers (licensees) and their processors to obtain explicit consumer consent, known as "opt-in," before using personal data for purposes beyond the core insurance transaction, such as selling data, targeted advertising, or joint marketing of financial products. Personal data is broadly defined to include identifiers, financial information, online activity, and even characteristics like racial or ethnic origin. Consumers are granted rights to access, correct, and delete their personal data, and insurers must provide clear privacy notices detailing these rights and how data is collected and shared. The bill also prohibits retaliation against consumers for exercising their privacy rights and mandates that insurers provide specific reasons for adverse underwriting decisions, preventing denial of insurance based solely on previous owners' loss history or data from processors without further verification. Violations can lead to civil lawsuits by consumers, with potential for significant damages, and enforcement by the Commissioner of Insurance, who can impose penalties and treat violations as unfair or deceptive practices in the insurance business.

bills.title: ID H0618 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes new transparency requirements for fire insurers in Idaho, requiring them to submit complete rate filings, including details about any wildfire risk models or scoring methods used, to the director of the department of insurance at least sixty days before they take effect. Insurers must also make information readily accessible on their public websites regarding premium discounts or adjustments available to policyholders who take property-specific or community-level wildfire mitigation actions, along with the process for appealing a wildfire risk score. Furthermore, insurers will be obligated to provide annual written notices to policyholders and applicants detailing applicable mitigation discounts, their wildfire risk score, and any other wildfire risk classifications used in underwriting, pricing, or surcharging premiums, including a plain-language explanation of how these scores are determined and the impact of mitigation efforts. The department of insurance will collect annual data from fire insurers on wildfire-related exposures and claims, and by January 1, 2027, will make aggregated, anonymized data and general insurance information available to consumers on its website, with the department also empowered to create further rules to implement these provisions. The bill also declares an emergency, making its provisions effective on July 1, 2026.

bills.title: IL HB3799 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends the Illinois Insurance Code to introduce new requirements for insurance companies regarding cancellation notices and climate risk disclosure. The bill extends the notice period for policy cancellations, mandating that for the first 60 days of coverage, cancellation notices must be mailed at least 30 days prior to the effective date, and after 60 days, notices must be mailed at least 60 days in advance. For nonpayment of premiums, a 10-day notice remains in effect. The bill also creates a new Climate Risk Disclosure Article that applies to insurance companies licensed in Illinois under Classes 2 and 3 that write $100 million or more in annual premiums. These companies will be required to participate in climate surveys issued by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) when directed by the Illinois Department of Insurance. The purpose of this new article is to enhance transparency about how insurers manage climate-related risks and to clarify the department's authority to require companies to participate in climate surveys. The monetary threshold for applicability can be adjusted by rule, providing flexibility for future implementation. Additionally, the bill includes provisions for market conduct actions, examinations, and potential penalties for non-compliance, with fines of up to $10,000 per day for certain violations.

bills.title: MI HB5383 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the Insurance Code of 1956 by amending Section 4705, primarily to update references from "commissioner" to "director of the department" and make other technical changes. The bill clarifies the process for a Special Purpose Financial Entity (SPFC), which is a type of company that can insure or reinsure risks for its counterparty, to obtain a limited certificate of authority to operate in Michigan. Key requirements for an SPFC include holding management meetings in the state, maintaining its principal business address in Michigan, designating a registered agent, and providing detailed documentation of its insurance securitization transactions to the director of the department. The bill also outlines the required content of an SPFC's organizational documents, including details about its incorporators, principal office, name, purpose, management structure, capital stock, and term of existence. It further specifies that directors of an SPFC may have limited personal liability for breaches of fiduciary duty, with exceptions for bad faith, intentional misconduct, or improper personal benefit. The bill details the review process by the director of the department before granting a certificate, which includes assessing the SPFC's financial condition, management expertise, plan of operation, and compliance with securities laws. Additionally, it adjusts application and annual renewal fees for SPFCs and clarifies the renewal process.

bills.title: MI HB5386 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies reporting and inquiry requirements for captive insurance companies, which are specialized insurance companies formed to insure the risks of their parent company or a group of companies. Key changes include requiring captive insurers to submit audited financial statements prepared by an independent public accountant within five months of their fiscal year-end, and clarifying that the director, rather than the commissioner, will handle inquiries and may prescribe the format for interim reports. The bill also adjusts the deadline for annual renewal fees to be paid within 90 days after the captive insurance company's fiscal year ends, and updates various references from "commissioner" to "director" and "office" to "department" to reflect current administrative structures. Additionally, it specifies that the director may employ legal counsel as needed for responsibilities under this chapter and clarifies that confidentiality provisions do not extend to final examination reports for captive insurance companies formed as risk retention groups.

bills.title: MI HB5385 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the requirements for captive insurance companies seeking a limited certificate of authority to operate in Michigan, primarily by changing the term "commissioner" to "director" throughout the relevant section of the insurance code, indicating a shift in regulatory oversight. It clarifies that these companies can offer various insurance coverages but are specifically prohibited from insuring "first-dollar" worker's compensation, long-term care, critical care, personal automobile, or homeowners insurance. The bill also adjusts the application fee for a limited certificate of authority from $10,000 to $5,000 and allows the director to use internal resources for application review at a $2,700 fee, in addition to the option of retaining outside legal, financial, and examination services. Furthermore, it updates the renewal period for the limited certificate of authority from March 1st to 90 days after the company's fiscal year ends, provided the company remains in good standing.

bills.title: OK SB1913 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a comprehensive dispute resolution system for insurance claims, requiring insurers to engage in mediation for certain residential and automobile insurance disputes when requested by a policyholder or assignee, provided civil litigation has not yet begun and the claim has gone through the Insurance Department's consumer complaint process. It also mandates that insurers provide policyholders with a "Homeowners Claims Bill of Rights" within fourteen days of receiving a claim, outlining their rights and advising them on steps to take during the claims process, and requires insurers to provide detailed estimates of loss to policyholders within seven days of generating them. Furthermore, the bill enhances penalties for violations of insurance laws, increases the maximum civil penalty for certain violations to $10,000, and allows the Insurance Commissioner to order restitution to damaged parties, while also clarifying rules around premium discounts for disaster-resistant construction and restricting insurers from canceling or non-renewing homeowner policies based solely on the age of the roof or a limited number of weather-related claims, with specific provisions for roof inspections and appeals for drone-based claim denials.

bills.title: FL H0943 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the operations of the Citizens Property Insurance Corporation, primarily by establishing new rules for commercial property insurance and refining its clearinghouse program. Key provisions include limiting Citizens' ability to issue or renew commercial residential and nonresidential policies if a surplus lines insurer offers comparable coverage at a price not more than 20% higher, and in such cases, Citizens may impose an "equalization adjustment" (a temporary policy-term adjustment to make costs comparable) to reflect the difference in price. The bill also mandates the creation of a personal lines clearinghouse and, by January 1, 2027, a commercial lines clearinghouse to facilitate offers of coverage from authorized insurers and approved surplus lines clearinghouse insurers, aiming to divert policyholders to the private market. It defines terms like "approved surplus lines clearinghouse insurer" and "comparable coverage," and outlines procedures for how these clearinghouses will operate, including the potential for a commercial lines clearinghouse administrator to charge fees and share policy information. The bill also specifies when applicants and policyholders are ineligible for Citizens coverage due to offers from other insurers and clarifies the rights and responsibilities of insurance agents involved in the clearinghouse process, while also authorizing the Office of Insurance Regulation to review the program's operational processes and recommend temporary measures if solvency risks are identified.

bills.title: MN HF4137 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires the Commissioner to create new rules for the State Building Code that incorporate standards for roof hardening, specifically based on the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety's (IIBHS) 2025 FORTIFIED Roof High Wind Standard with Hail Supplement, and these standards must be adapted to Minnesota's climate. The IIBHS is an independent research organization that tests building products and systems for their ability to withstand natural disasters. The FORTIFIED Roof standard is designed to improve a roof's resilience against high winds and hail, and this bill mandates that the new rules cover both new construction and the re-roofing of existing buildings. To help develop these rules, the Commissioner must assemble a technical advisory group that will include a representative from the IIBHS.

bills.title: OR SB1540 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires insurance companies that use models to assess wildfire risk for properties to submit detailed descriptions of these models, including how they incorporate community-level and property-specific mitigation actions, to the Director of the Department of Consumer and Business Services. Mitigation actions are defined as scientifically based efforts to reduce wildfire risk, such as forest treatments, fire-resistant landscaping, or community certifications. Insurers must also provide premium discounts or other incentives to policyholders who undertake property-specific mitigation or whose properties are near community-level mitigation efforts. Furthermore, insurers must clearly post information about available discounts and the process for appealing risk classifications or disputes over incentives on their websites, and provide this information to applicants and policyholders, explaining how their property's risk score was determined and what actions could lead to a better score or lower premium. Policyholders will have the right to appeal their wildfire risk classification or dispute the amount of any discount or incentive offered. The bill takes effect on July 1, 2027, and applies to policies issued or renewed on or after that date, with provisions for rule adoption before the operative date.

bills.title: NY A10529 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill requires property and casualty insurance companies operating in New York to annually report to the Department of Financial Services (DFS) information about multifamily residential buildings, which are defined as properties with two or more dwelling units. The reports, due by March 1st each year for the preceding calendar year, must include details on premiums collected and claims paid, along with any other information the DFS deems necessary in consultation with the Division of Housing and Community Renewal. The DFS will then create a public database to display this information, though any data considered trade secrets or sensitive commercial information will be aggregated, de-identified, and kept confidential, meaning it will not be individually published or subject to public disclosure laws.

bills.title: FL S0990 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a comprehensive legal framework for protected cell captive insurance companies in Florida, providing detailed regulations for their formation, operation, and governance. The legislation defines a protected cell captive insurance company as a special type of insurance company that can create separate protected cells to insure risks for different participants, with each cell having its own distinct pool of assets and liabilities that are legally segregated from other cells and the company's general account. Key provisions include requiring these companies to be incorporated as stock, mutual, nonprofit, or limited liability companies, mandating strict accounting and administrative procedures to keep each cell's assets and liabilities separate, and establishing specific requirements for creating and managing protected cells. The bill outlines requirements for capital and surplus, details how assets and liabilities can be attributed to different cells, specifies reporting and notification obligations, and provides guidelines for potential scenarios such as cell conversions, mergers, and insolvency. The legislation aims to provide a clear regulatory structure that allows for more flexible and innovative insurance arrangements while maintaining robust financial protections for participants and maintaining oversight by the Office of Insurance Regulation.

bills.title: IN HB1260 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill makes several changes to Indiana insurance laws, including requiring entities to report fraudulent insurance acts to the department of insurance or the National Insurance Crime Bureau, and providing immunity from civil or criminal liability for such reports made without malice or bad faith. It also establishes a fee schedule for accessing unrestricted data from the all-payer claims database, with different rates for various entities. The bill modifies rules regarding notice of material changes in insurance policies, including extending the notice period for non-renewal of residential policies to 60 days and requiring insurers to provide specific information when using aerial images for coverage decisions. It allows co-owners of certain condominiums to obtain property and casualty insurance through a master policy or individual policies, and requires insurers and health maintenance organizations to provide insurance producers with a list of their contracted providers. The bill clarifies that the prohibition on denying or discriminating in pricing Medicare supplement policies applies to applicants who apply within a month before or after their birthday. Additionally, it grants the insurance commissioner authority to establish and operate a health benefit exchange and mandates an annual report on the fee schedule for data access. Finally, it makes changes related to the property and casualty insurance and guaranty association law, including allowing the insurance commissioner to waive merger or consolidation requirements for farm mutual insurance companies in emergency situations and clarifying that policies covering liability exceeding a self-insured retention amount are considered commercial umbrella or excess liability policies for uninsured and underinsured motorist coverage.

bills.title: HI SB3000 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill authorizes the Attorney General to sue on behalf of the state, as "parens patriae" (a Latin term meaning "parent of the country," signifying the government's role as protector of those who cannot protect themselves), any "responsible party" – defined as a large company involved in extracting, producing, marketing, or selling fossil fuel products that has significant business in Hawaii – to recover costs and losses incurred due to climate-related disasters. It also allows the Hawaii Property Insurance Association (an "insurer of last resort" that provides coverage when the private market fails), the Hawaii Hurricane Relief Fund (a state-funded entity for hurricane insurance), or any private insurer licensed in Hawaii to sue responsible parties for their own climate-related costs and losses. Funds recovered from these lawsuits will go into a new Climate Disaster Special Fund, which will then distribute the money in a specific order: first to policyholders for restitution, then to the Hawaii Hurricane Relief Fund, then to cover court costs and attorney fees, and finally to fund mitigation and adaptation projects. The bill also makes changes to how insurance rates are determined, requiring them to account for any proceeds recovered from these climate-related lawsuits, and adjusts the operational plans for the Hawaii Property Insurance Association and the Hawaii Hurricane Relief Fund to similarly consider such recoveries. This legislation is set to take effect on July 1, 2050.

bills.title: OK HB3696 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill modifies Oklahoma's insurance laws concerning property and casualty rates, primarily by changing the process for how insurance companies file and use these rates. It introduces a new system where insurers must file their rates, along with supporting documents like rating manuals and supplementary information, with the Insurance Commissioner. Once filed, insurers can generally use these rates immediately, though the Commissioner has a limited timeframe (initially 30 days, extendable for another 30 for good cause) to review and potentially disapprove them if they don't comply with the law. The bill also outlines specific exemptions from these filing requirements for certain large commercial policyholders and for smaller insurers in the residential property market, with conditions for when even these exempted insurers must file. Importantly, the bill repeals existing laws that presumed a competitive market and governed rate filings, and it establishes procedures for hearings and appeals if a rate is disapproved, as well as for insureds who are aggrieved by a filing. The Oklahoma Insurance Department will also track reasons for rate disapprovals and requests for additional information. The bill aims to streamline the rate-filing process while maintaining oversight and consumer protection, with an effective date of November 1, 2026.

bills.title: IA HF2511 (2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a framework for statewide resilience planning in Iowa, mandating the development, implementation, and regular updating of an Iowa State Resilience Plan. The plan, to be created by the Iowa Flood Center in collaboration with various state agencies and stakeholders by December 31, 2028, aims to protect against loss of life and property, incorporate future hazard conditions into state programs, identify priority resilience projects, and guide state and local policy. Key components of the plan include a statewide risk assessment detailing vulnerabilities to flooding, water supply changes, and other natural hazards, an inventory of "critical assets" (which are essential infrastructure and community resources like transportation networks, utilities, schools, and natural areas) and "regionally significant assets" (critical assets serving multiple jurisdictions), a methodology for prioritizing resilience projects based on benefits and regional factors, a list of ongoing and proposed projects with their costs, and recommendations for voluntary risk reduction methods and policy changes. The Iowa Flood Center will report on the plan's progress and provide updated recommendations every two years, and the plan itself will be updated every five years.

bills.title: CA SB326 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the California Wildfire Mitigation Strategic Planning Act, which creates a comprehensive framework for addressing wildfire risks in California. The bill requires the Deputy Director of Community Wildfire Preparedness and Mitigation to develop several key planning documents: a Wildfire Risk Mitigation Planning Framework (every three years), a Wildfire Risk Baseline and Forecast (every three years), and an annual Wildfire Mitigation Scenarios Report. These documents will help quantitatively evaluate and coordinate wildfire risk mitigation efforts across various agencies and stakeholders. The bill also accelerates the implementation of "zone zero" regulations, which require creating an ember-resistant zone within 5 feet of a structure in high fire hazard areas. Additionally, the bill provides funding for local agencies to hire wildfire inspectors and implement fire prevention activities, and it expands building standards to apply to reconstructed buildings in wildfire-affected areas. The legislation aims to address the increasing wildfire risks caused by climate change, drought, and historic fire suppression policies by creating a more strategic, data-driven approach to wildfire prevention and mitigation. The bill recognizes the complex challenges of modern wildfires and seeks to improve coordination between various entities responsible for managing wildfire risks in California.

bills.title: CA SB629 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill (known as the Keeping Communities Safe from Wildfire Act of 2025) expands California's approach to identifying and managing fire hazard areas by requiring the State Fire Marshal to include new factors when designating fire hazard severity zones. These factors now include areas that have experienced significant wildfires (burning at least 1,000 acres, destroying more than 10 structures, or causing fatalities), as well as areas at risk for urban conflagrations where structures can act as fuel sources. The bill introduces a new concept of "post-wildfire safety areas" for lands within wildfire perimeters, which will trigger the application of state fire protection standards 30 days after the State Fire Marshal transmits a map of the area to local agencies. Local agencies will be required to post notices about these areas and update their general plan safety elements to address the risks in these newly designated zones. The bill aims to improve wildfire risk mapping by considering how buildings and agricultural lands can contribute to fire spread, and it requires the State Fire Marshal to publish the methodology used to create fire hazard maps at least 60 days before finalizing them. Additionally, the bill recognizes that homes built to wildland-urban interface (WUI) building standards are up to 40 percent less likely to be destroyed by wildfires and seeks to expand the application of these standards to more areas.

bills.title: NH HB1273 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill renames the Coastal Resilience Incentive Zone (CRIZ) to the Flood Resilience Incentive Zone (FRIZ), expanding the scope of flood mitigation efforts beyond coastal areas to include inland flood-prone regions. The bill allows municipalities to establish FRIZ zones using tools like the New Hampshire Flood Hazards Viewer and provides a comprehensive set of qualifying improvements, including structural elevation, flood-resistant materials, water management practices, and system modifications that reduce flood damage. Municipalities can now create special nonlapsing funds and revolving investment funds to support flood resilience efforts, with the ability to collect a municipal flood resilience fee of up to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed property value. The bill also introduces property tax credits for flood resilience investments, offering property owners between $100 and $1,000 annually for up to five years, with credits tied to the property itself rather than the owner.
