The Legislature recommends examining ways to broaden the Alabama Insurance Underwriting Association's Wind Pool, create state-administered contingency funds for post-disaster recovery, and strengthen construction standards in high-risk areas. Additionally, the resolution suggests extending the work of the Alabama Coastal Commercial Insurance Joint Interim Study Commission to continue refining solutions and assessing market conditions. Ultimately, the resolution expresses the Legislature's commitment to fostering an environment that supports economic and insurance stability in coastal Alabama, with a formal commitment to continued study and stakeholder engagement.

bills.title: TX HB345 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a comprehensive framework for the appraisal process in residential property insurance policies in Texas, providing detailed guidelines for how insurers and policyholders should resolve disputes over loss amounts. The bill applies to most types of residential property insurers, excluding the Texas Windstorm Insurance Association and commercial insurance policies, and outlines a structured process where either the policyholder or insurer can demand an appraisal if they cannot agree on the loss amount. The appraisal process requires each party to select a competent and impartial appraiser within 20 days, who then attempt to determine the loss amount within 30 days, with the option to extend this deadline by an additional 30 days. If the initial appraisers cannot agree, they must select an impartial umpire, who will then choose between the two proposed loss amounts or select an amount in between. The bill specifies that appraisal expenses are split equally, with each party paying their own appraiser, and establishes provisions for terminating the appraisal process if deadlines are not met. Importantly, the appraisal amount becomes binding, but does not affect other policy terms or prevent future legal action. The bill will take effect on September 1, 2025, and will apply to insurance policies delivered or renewed on or after January 1, 2026.

bills.title: NV AB437 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Fair Access to Insurance Requirements (FAIR) Plan Association, a nonprofit unincorporated public entity designed to provide property and commercial property insurance coverage when such coverage is not otherwise available from authorized insurers in Nevada. The Association will be governed by a 12-member Board of Directors appointed by the Governor, representing various insurance industry stakeholders, consumers, and fire chiefs. The FAIR Plan will offer property insurance policies up to $750,000 and commercial property insurance policies up to $5,000,000, with specific eligibility requirements including a fire risk assessment and documentation of at least three coverage declinations from other insurers. All authorized insurers offering property or commercial property insurance in the state will automatically be members of the Association and will be required to share expenses, income, and losses on a proportional basis. The plan includes provisions for fee collection, policy issuance through insurance producers, and Commissioner of Insurance oversight, with the goal of ensuring fair access to insurance for properties that have difficulty obtaining coverage through traditional insurance markets. The bill also establishes a mechanism for the Commissioner to review and approve the Association's plan of operation, with the ability to make changes or impose regulations if necessary to meet the legislative intent.

bills.title: ME LD1 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes several new programs and funds aimed at increasing storm preparedness and resilience for Maine's communities, homes, and infrastructure in response to recent winter storm damage. The bill creates a Home Resiliency Program within the Bureau of Insurance, which will provide grants to homeowners to perform projects that make homes more resistant to extreme weather events, with $15 million initially transferred to the program's fund. It also establishes the Safeguarding Tomorrow through Ongoing Risk Mitigation Revolving Loan Fund within the Maine Emergency Management Agency to provide state matching funds for federal disaster and hazard mitigation loans to support municipal and tribal infrastructure projects. Additionally, the bill creates a State Resilience Office within the Maine Office of Community Affairs to coordinate resilience policies, administer existing programs like the Community Resilience Partnership Program, and develop new resilience initiatives. The State Resilience Fund will support this work, with an initial transfer of $9.6 million and an annual transfer of $1.755 million beginning in fiscal year 2027-28. The bill includes provisions for updating flood mapping, improving emergency communication systems, and increasing participation in the National Flood Insurance Program, with the ultimate goal of helping Maine communities better prepare for and mitigate risks from extreme weather events.

bills.title: GA SB68 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill introduces comprehensive changes to civil practice, evidentiary matters, damages, and liability in tort actions in Georgia. The bill covers multiple key areas: First, it revises rules about arguing noneconomic damages in court, specifying that lawyers can only argue such damages after evidence is closed and must relate their arguments rationally to the evidence. Second, it modifies procedural rules for filing answers in civil cases, creating more specific timelines for defendants to respond to lawsuits and establishing new provisions for staying discovery during motions to dismiss. Third, the bill creates new standards for voluntary dismissal of civil actions, including limitations on repeated dismissals. Fourth, it establishes new regulations around attorney's fees, preventing duplicate recovery of fees and restricting the use of contingent fee agreements as proof of reasonable fees. The bill also introduces significant changes to negligent security liability, defining specific conditions under which property owners can be held liable for third-party criminal actions, and creating detailed standards for determining foreseeability and apportioning fault. Additionally, the bill allows more flexibility in using seat belt non-usage as evidence in civil trials and establishes new rules for recovering medical expenses in personal injury cases, including allowing evidence of insurance payments and healthcare billing practices. The legislation aims to provide more clarity and structure to civil litigation processes in Georgia.

bills.title: GA SB69 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes comprehensive regulations for third-party litigation financing in Georgia, creating a new legal framework to govern how companies can provide funding to plaintiffs in legal cases. The bill requires litigation financiers to register with the Department of Banking and Finance, prohibits entities with foreign government affiliations from providing such financing, and imposes strict rules on how these financial arrangements can be structured. Key provisions include mandating that litigation financing agreements be in writing, contain specific consumer disclosures, and prohibit financiers from influencing legal strategy or decision-making in the cases they fund. The legislation limits the amount a litigation financier can recover to a proportional share of the plaintiff's proceeds, requires indemnification for potential legal costs, and provides consumers with a five-day right to cancel the financing agreement without penalty. Violations of these regulations can result in felony charges with potential imprisonment of one to five years and fines up to $10,000. The bill also amends existing laws to allow discovery of litigation financing agreements in civil cases and modifies rules about the admissibility of seat belt non-use as evidence in motor vehicle liability cases. Most provisions will become effective on January 1, 2026, with some sections taking effect immediately upon the Governor's approval.

bills.title: HI SCR57 (2025)
bills.summary: This resolution urges the United States Congress to establish a National Reinsurance Program to address the growing insurance crisis caused by increasingly frequent and severe natural disasters like hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and earthquakes. The resolution highlights that major insurers are withdrawing from high-risk areas, leading to skyrocketing premiums and limited coverage for homeowners, businesses, and agricultural producers. The proposed national reinsurance program would function as a public-private partnership with key characteristics including actuarial soundness, optional state participation, and clearly defined loss thresholds that protect taxpayers while maintaining private market involvement. Additionally, the resolution recommends that Congress provide mitigation incentives such as premium discounts, tax credits, and grants to encourage investments in disaster-resistant infrastructure. It also suggests that the President, Treasury Secretary, Federal Insurance Office, and Federal Emergency Management Agency develop interim measures to stabilize insurance markets, such as expanding access to federal risk modeling and offering financial incentives for insurers to return to high-risk markets. The ultimate goal is to promote economic security, sustainable homeownership, and improved disaster preparedness while reducing the need for extensive post-disaster federal aid.

bills.title: HI SCR198 (2025)
bills.summary: This concurrent resolution encourages Hawaii insurers and the Hawaii Property Insurance Association (HPIA) to pursue legal action against fossil fuel companies that knowingly misled the public about their products' role in climate change. The resolution highlights the increasing climate-related disasters in Hawaii, such as hurricanes, droughts, and the 2023 Maui wildfires, which have destabilized the state's insurance industry and led to rising premiums. Drawing parallels to previous legal actions against industries like tobacco and opioid manufacturers, the resolution suggests that insurers should use subrogation claims (a legal process where an insurer seeks reimbursement from responsible parties after paying a claim) to recover costs and potentially reduce insurance expenses for local residents. The resolution emphasizes Hawaii's state interest in protecting citizens from climate disasters and misleading corporate practices, and directs certified copies of the resolution to be sent to key state and national officials, including the Governor, Attorney General, and Insurance Commissioner.

bills.title: AR SB92 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill provides an appropriation for the Department of Transformation and Shared Services for the 2025-2026 fiscal year, detailing funding and staffing allocations across multiple divisions including Statewide Shared Services, Building Authority, Information Systems, Geographic Information Systems, and the Office of Property Risk. The bill establishes maximum employee numbers and salary rates for various positions, and appropriates funds for regular salaries, extra help, personal service matching, operating expenses, and specific program needs. Key appropriations include $14.9 million for Statewide Shared Services operations, $90.6 million for Division of Information Systems operations, and $134.9 million for the Office of Property Risk, which covers operational expenses and claims. The bill also includes special language provisions allowing for fund transfers between certain accounts with approval from the Chief Fiscal Officer and Legislative Council, and emphasizes compliance with state procurement, accounting, and budgetary laws. An emergency clause is included to ensure the act can take effect on July 1, 2025, highlighting the critical nature of these appropriations for government operations.

bills.title: AR SB481 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill creates a comprehensive State Captive Insurance Program to provide property insurance coverage for public schools, state-supported institutions of higher education, and state-owned properties. The legislation establishes a captive insurance company within the Department of Transformation and Shared Services to manage and administer a more sustainable insurance system. Key provisions include mandatory participation for public schools and state agencies receiving state appropriations, a prohibition on using public insurance adjusters, and the creation of a State Captive Insurance Program Trust Fund. The bill aims to address the current crisis in property insurance by restructuring existing insurance programs, implementing stricter risk management practices, and providing more stable and cost-effective coverage. The program will be overseen by the Secretary of the Department of Transformation and Shared Services, with annual reporting to the Legislative Council and mandatory legislative oversight. The bill includes provisions for maintaining an optimal reserve balance, establishing premium rates based on actuarial assessments, and ensuring transparency in the insurance program's operations. An emergency clause is included, highlighting the urgent need to address rising insurance costs and instability in the current property insurance system for public institutions.

bills.title: CO HB1205 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the Fair Access to Insurance Requirements (FAIR) Plan Association in Colorado, clarifying its legal status and operational guidelines. The bill explicitly states that the FAIR Plan Association is not a state agency, government entity, or instrumentality, and any debts or obligations are solely the association's responsibility, with no state financial backing. The association's primary purpose remains providing property insurance coverage when commercial insurance is unavailable. The bill adds provisions that grant immunity to the association, its board, members, and regulators for actions taken in the performance of their duties. It also limits policyholder legal remedies against the association to breach of contract or breach of good faith, setting strict standards for such claims. Specifically, for a bad faith claim, a policyholder must prove the association acted unreasonably and knew or recklessly disregarded such unreasonableness, with damages limited to compensatory losses and punitive damages only available in cases of fraud, malice, or willful misconduct. The bill further specifies that successful policyholder claims can result in attorney fees, while frivolous claims could lead to the association receiving cost and fee awards.

bills.title: IA HF957 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill is a comprehensive piece of legislation that addresses multiple areas related to disaster recovery, insurance, and financial assistance in Iowa. The bill contains eight divisions that cover: (1) creating a natural hazard mitigation financing program to provide loans for projects mitigating natural disaster risks, (2) modifying the disaster recovery housing assistance program to provide more flexible financial aid to homeowners and renters affected by disasters, (3) establishing a tax exemption for state disaster recovery new housing grants, (4) regulating post-loss assignments of insurance benefits to residential contractors, (5) licensing and regulating public, independent, and staff adjusters, (6) licensing and regulating appraisers and umpires in insurance claims, (7) modifying the Iowa economic emergency fund to allow annual appropriations for disaster response, and (8) making specific appropriations from the economic emergency fund for nuisance property remediation and disaster recovery housing assistance. The bill introduces detailed requirements for licensing, professional conduct, and penalties for adjusters, appraisers, and umpires, with provisions designed to protect consumers and ensure fair practices in insurance claims processing. It also provides mechanisms for financial assistance and loan programs to help communities and individuals recover from natural disasters, while establishing strict guidelines for how these funds can be used and who can access them.

bills.title: IA HF982 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill comprehensively reforms several areas of insurance, disaster recovery, and state financial management in Iowa. The bill creates a new Natural Hazard Mitigation Financing Program to provide loans for projects that mitigate natural disaster impacts, such as flood control or zoning changes. It establishes detailed regulations for public adjusters, appraisers, and umpires in insurance claims, including licensing requirements, standards of conduct, and penalties for misconduct. The bill also modifies the Disaster Recovery Housing Assistance Program to provide more flexible financial assistance to homeowners and renters affected by natural disasters. Additionally, the legislation allows the state to appropriate up to 10% of the Iowa Economic Emergency Fund annually for disaster response and recovery activities, with approval from the governor and executive council. The bill includes tax provisions for disaster recovery grants and appropriates $2 million to a nuisance property remediation fund and $11.6 million to the disaster recovery housing assistance fund for the 2024-2025 fiscal year. These changes aim to improve Iowa's preparedness, response, and recovery capabilities during and after natural disasters while providing stronger consumer protections in insurance claims processes.

bills.title: IA HF1012 (2025)
bills.summary:
This bill introduces comprehensive legislation covering multiple areas related to disaster recovery, insurance, and state emergency funds. Here's a summary:

This bill creates a Natural Hazard Mitigation Financing Program to provide loans to eligible entities for projects that mitigate natural hazard impacts, such as flooding, drought, and wildfires. The program will be jointly managed by the Department of Homeland Security and Emergency Management and the Iowa Finance Authority, with a dedicated revolving loan fund. The bill also modifies the Disaster Recovery Housing Assistance Program to provide more flexible financial assistance to homeowners and renters affected by disasters, and introduces new regulations for public adjusters, appraisers, and umpires in insurance claim processes. Additionally, the bill establishes a mechanism for the Iowa Economic Emergency Fund to appropriate up to 10% of its balance annually for disaster response and recovery activities, contingent on a gubernatorial disaster emergency proclamation. The legislation includes provisions for licensing, conduct standards, and penalties for adjusters, appraisers, and umpires, and makes appropriations of $2 million to the nuisance property remediation assistance fund and $11.6 million to the disaster recovery housing assistance fund for the fiscal year 2024-2025.

bills.title: AR SB237 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill amends Arkansas law concerning the licensing and regulation of captive insurance companies, making several key changes. It modifies the definition of "association" by removing the requirement of continuous existence for at least one year, eliminates certain definitions related to the Commissioner and Department, and introduces a new provisional licensing mechanism that allows the Insurance Commissioner to issue a temporary license to captive insurance companies under specific conditions. The bill reduces capital and surplus requirements for various types of captive insurance companies, such as association and industrial insured captive insurers, and simplifies dividend payment rules. Additionally, it establishes new provisions for foreign or alien insurers to redomesticate to Arkansas, including tax incentives and reporting requirements. The bill also modifies examination frequencies for pure captive insurance companies, creates a new tax credit for Arkansas employees, and adds a section detailing potential violations that could result in suspension or revocation of a captive insurance company's certificate of authority. These changes aim to make Arkansas more attractive for captive insurance companies by providing more flexible regulatory frameworks and potential financial benefits.

bills.title: AR HB1821 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill creates a comprehensive State Captive Insurance Program to provide a more sustainable system of property insurance for public schools, state-supported institutions of higher education, and state-owned properties. The legislation establishes a captive insurance company within the Department of Transformation and Shared Services to centralize and streamline property insurance coverage for these entities. Key provisions include mandatory participation for public schools and state agencies receiving state appropriations, a prohibition on using public insurance adjusters, and the creation of a dedicated Trust Fund to manage the program's finances. The bill aims to address rising insurance costs and market instability by consolidating insurance programs, implementing strict oversight, and creating a more actuarially sound approach to managing property risks. The program will be governed by a complex set of rules requiring annual reporting to the Legislative Council, maintaining a specific reserve balance, and ensuring transparency in its operations. An emergency clause highlights the urgent need to address escalating insurance costs and provide more predictable coverage for public institutions. The program is set to begin operations by December 1, 2025, with a phased transition of existing insurance programs into the new system.

bills.title: KS HB2334 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill enacts the Kansas protected cell captive insurance company act, introducing a comprehensive framework for creating and operating protected cell captive insurance companies. The legislation allows one or more sponsors to form a protected cell captive insurance company, which can be structured as a stock insurer, mutual corporation, nonprofit corporation, or limited liability company. Key provisions include establishing distinct rules for creating and managing protected cells, which are separate accounts within the insurance company where assets and liabilities can be segregated and insulated from the company's general account. The bill provides detailed definitions and requirements for participants, participant contracts, and the financial management of these protected cells, including how assets and liabilities can be attributed and managed. Additionally, the bill makes several technical amendments to existing insurance laws, including reducing premium tax rates for insurance companies, updating licensing requirements for insurance agents and public adjusters, and authorizing insurers to file certain travel insurance policies under different insurance lines. The legislation aims to provide more flexibility and regulatory clarity for captive insurance companies operating in Kansas, while maintaining consumer protections and financial oversight.

bills.title: TX SB2571 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill modifies the funding mechanisms for the Texas Windstorm Insurance Association (TWIA), establishing a new framework for handling catastrophic losses before and after January 1, 2026. Specifically, the bill creates a new Subchapter B-2 that outlines how the association will pay excess losses and operating expenses for catastrophe years occurring after December 31, 2025. The new provisions allow the association to pay insured losses from its reserves and a catastrophe reserve trust fund, with a cap of 33 percent on member assessments. If losses exceed these sources, the association can issue Class 1 public securities to be repaid within 14 years. The bill also provides flexibility for members to purchase reinsurance to cover potential assessments and establishes rules for how and when public securities can be issued. Additionally, the bill makes conforming changes to various sections of the Insurance Code to align with the new funding structure, repeals older subchapters, and ensures a smooth transition between the existing and new funding mechanisms. The changes are designed to provide a more structured and predictable approach to funding TWIA's losses, with the new system taking full effect on January 1, 2026.

bills.title: NM SB83 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill creates the Innovation in State Government Fund, a new non-reverting fund in the state treasury that will be administered by the Department of Finance and Administration. The fund can receive distributions, appropriations, gifts, grants, and donations, and its money can be appropriated to state agencies for specific climate-related purposes, including creating master plans to achieve net-zero emissions (fully offsetting greenhouse gas emissions), implementing sustainable economic policies, providing technical support for climate change-related grants, and reducing barriers to climate change policy implementation. The bill defines key terms like "greenhouse gas" (gases that contribute to the greenhouse effect) and "sustainable economic policies" (policies promoting job creation, economic diversification, and long-term economic growth). Agencies receiving funds must submit an annual report by June 30 detailing how the money will be spent and justify any proposed program changes. Additionally, the fund can be used to support the general fund in the event of potential budget shortfalls, but only after other reserve funds have been exhausted and only in the amount necessary to meet general fund appropriations.

bills.title: NM SB33 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Wildfire Prepared Act, which creates a comprehensive approach to wildfire prevention and property protection in New Mexico. The legislation forms a Fire Planning Task Force comprising representatives from various state agencies, local governments, and wildfire experts, tasked with identifying high-risk areas, developing building codes and defensible space requirements, and creating wildfire preparedness standards. The bill introduces a Wildfire Prepared Program within the state's Energy, Minerals and Natural Resources Department to provide technical assistance, conduct property assessments, and award grants to political subdivisions and qualified entities to help property owners make their residences more fire-resistant. A new Wildfire Prepared Fund is established to support these efforts, funded through distributions, appropriations, gifts, and grants. The program aims to reduce wildfire risks by promoting noncombustible building materials, creating protective buffers, and implementing fire mitigation strategies, with at least half of the grant money dedicated to directly assisting eligible property owners in making their homes more wildfire-resistant. Importantly, the bill explicitly states that it does not create additional legal rights or guarantee specific benefits, serving primarily as a proactive approach to wildfire prevention and community protection.

bills.title: MN SF2205 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a task force to evaluate and provide recommendations on homeowners and commercial property insurance affordability and sustainability in Minnesota. The task force will consist of 13 members appointed by various organizations and legislative leaders, including representatives from commerce, housing, insurance, and real estate sectors. The group will be charged with identifying ways to strengthen and stabilize the insurance industry by examining issues such as risk mitigation, natural hazard resilience, liability laws, insurance cost transparency, reinsurance markets, and factors that increase claim costs like climate change and inflation. The task force must hold its first meeting by September 15, 2025, elect leadership, and develop a meeting schedule. By February 15, 2026, they are required to submit a comprehensive report to relevant state commissioners and legislative committees, including their findings, recommendations, and potential draft legislation. The task force will be provided $200,000 in administrative funding and will automatically expire after submitting its report. The goal is to address challenges in the insurance market that impact housing and small business infrastructure in Minnesota.

bills.title: MN HF2389 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill introduces several key changes to insurance regulations in Minnesota. It authorizes the commissioner of commerce to issue "data calls," which are written requests for specific information from insurance companies, and establishes new confidentiality and usage rules for these data calls. The bill introduces requirements for insurance holding companies to file group capital calculations and participate in liquidity stress tests, which are financial assessments designed to evaluate the financial health and risk of insurance groups. The legislation also creates a comprehensive framework for "limited long-term care insurance," establishing detailed disclosure, performance, and consumer protection standards for these policies, including provisions on policy cancellation, benefit triggers, and nonforfeiture options. Additionally, the bill makes various modifications to automobile insurance regulations, such as adjusting how risks are distributed among insurance companies and clarifying participation ratios. The bill also includes technical changes to existing insurance statutes, refining definitions, updating language, and adding new provisions for regulatory oversight of insurance companies and groups. These changes aim to enhance financial transparency, consumer protection, and regulatory effectiveness in the insurance industry.

bills.title: GA HB691 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill creates the Georgia Resilience Office within the Georgia Emergency Management and Homeland Security Agency to enhance the state's disaster preparedness and recovery capabilities. The office will be governed by a 13-member board of directors, including state agency heads, legislative representatives, and local government representatives, with a primary focus on developing a comprehensive state-wide resilience plan. This plan will assess risks from extreme weather events, evaluate infrastructure vulnerabilities, identify potential mitigation strategies, and recommend policy improvements across various sectors like transportation, energy, healthcare, and agriculture. The office will be led by a chief resilience officer who will coordinate economic and community resilience efforts, hire staff, coordinate with federal and state agencies, establish a public website, and report annually to the General Assembly. The bill aims to improve Georgia's ability to prepare for, respond to, and recover from natural disasters by creating a dedicated office to research, plan, and implement resilience strategies, with the first comprehensive resilience plan due by December 31, 2026. The office will also have the authority to accept federal and private funding, provide grants for resilience research, and develop technical assistance resources for local governments.

bills.title: HI SCR155 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This concurrent resolution requests the Insurance Commissioner to produce a comprehensive report on Hawaii's private property insurance industry, addressing critical challenges posed by increasing natural disasters like hurricanes, wildfires, and volcanic eruptions. The resolution seeks a detailed analysis of the industry's current state, including factors driving premium increases, the financial health of insurers, and the potential risk of insurers withdrawing from the market. Specifically, the report is requested to provide information on the number of private property insurers, their financial performance, and an assessment of the likelihood of insurers ceasing operations in Hawaii. Additionally, the resolution asks the Insurance Commissioner to evaluate the State's capacity to enter the insurance market as a primary provider, including exploring the potential use of eminent domain to acquire private insurance companies if necessary. The report is to be submitted no later than twenty days before the 2026 Regular Session, with the goal of helping the Legislature understand and potentially mitigate the growing challenges in the property insurance market that could significantly impact homeowners and the state's economic stability.

bills.title: HI SR41 (2025)
bills.summary: This resolution urges the United States Congress to establish a National Reinsurance Program to address the growing insurance crisis caused by increasingly frequent and severe catastrophic natural disasters like hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and earthquakes. The resolution highlights that major insurers are withdrawing from high-risk areas, leading to reduced coverage and dramatically increased premiums that are harming homeowners, businesses, and agricultural producers. The proposed national program would serve as a financial backstop, operating as a public-private partnership with key features including actuarial soundness, state opt-in flexibility, and clearly defined loss thresholds to protect taxpayers while maintaining private market participation. Additionally, the resolution recommends that the federal government provide mitigation incentives such as premium discounts, tax credits, and grants to encourage disaster-resistant infrastructure investments. It also suggests that the President, Treasury Secretary, Federal Insurance Office, and Federal Emergency Management Agency develop interim measures to stabilize insurance markets, such as expanding access to federal risk modeling and offering financial incentives for insurers to return to high-risk markets. The ultimate goal is to promote economic security, sustainable homeownership, and disaster preparedness while reducing the need for costly post-disaster federal aid.

bills.title: HI SR178 (2025)
bills.summary: This resolution encourages Hawaii insurers and the Hawaii Property Insurance Association (HPIA) to pursue subrogation claims against fossil fuel companies that knowingly misled the public about their products' contributions to climate change. The resolution highlights the increasing climate-related risks facing Hawaii, including rising temperatures, more severe weather events, and devastating incidents like the 2023 Maui wildfires, which have destabilized the state's insurance industry and led to higher premiums. Drawing parallels to previous legal actions against industries like tobacco and opioid manufacturers, the resolution argues that polluters should be held financially responsible for the damages caused by their deceptive practices. The document emphasizes Hawaii's compelling state interest in protecting its citizens from climate disasters and maintaining an affordable insurance market. Specifically, the resolution calls on insurers and HPIA to use subrogation—a legal mechanism allowing insurers to recover claim payments from responsible third parties—to potentially reduce insurance costs for local residents by seeking compensation from companies that have contributed to climate change through misleading communications about their environmental impact.

bills.title: HI SR125 (2025)
bills.summary: This resolution requests the Insurance Commissioner to produce a comprehensive report on the state of the private property insurance industry in Hawaii, driven by concerns about increasing natural disasters like hurricanes, wildfires, and volcanic eruptions that are causing significant property damage and financial strain. The requested report must detail factors driving premium increases, particularly in high-risk areas, and explore the potential for a publicly administered insurance program. The resolution seeks specific information including the number of private property insurers, their financial performance (revenues, profits, losses), and an assessment of the likelihood that insurers might cease operations in Hawaii. Furthermore, the report is expected to provide recommendations on the state's potential alternatives if private insurers withdraw, including the unprecedented possibility of using eminent domain to acquire private insurance companies. The Insurance Commissioner is asked to submit this detailed report twenty days before the 2026 Regular Session, with the ultimate goal of helping the Legislature understand and potentially mitigate risks to homeowners and the broader insurance market in the face of increasing environmental and economic challenges.

bills.title: TX HB4532 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill requires residential property insurance providers in Texas to offer an optional endorsement that provides enhanced coverage for roof replacements meeting specific standards. Under the proposed legislation, insurers must provide policyholders with the choice to purchase an endorsement that allows roof replacements to be completed according to either the Institute for Business and Home Safety's fortified home roof standards or another standard defined by the insurer in the policy. The endorsement would be subject to an actuarially justified premium, though insurers are not mandated to charge extra. The bill applies to most residential property and fire insurance providers, excluding the Texas Windstorm Insurance Association, the FAIR Plan Association, and commercial insurance policies. The coverage is voluntary for policyholders and would only be available when an insured elects to purchase the additional endorsement. The insurance commissioner is authorized to adopt implementation rules, but cannot set or suggest specific premium rates. The bill will take effect on September 1, 2025, and will apply to insurance policies delivered, issued, or renewed on or after January 1, 2026, giving insurers and consumers time to prepare for the new option.

bills.title: IA SF591 (2025)
bills.summary:
This bill addresses multiple aspects of disaster recovery, insurance regulation, and state financial management. Here's a comprehensive summary:

This bill creates a comprehensive framework for natural hazard mitigation, disaster recovery, and insurance claims management in Iowa. The legislation establishes a Natural Hazard Mitigation Financing Program that allows the state to provide loans to eligible entities for projects designed to mitigate natural disaster risks, such as flood control structures, zoning changes, and building code enforcement. The bill introduces new licensing and regulatory requirements for public adjusters, independent adjusters, appraisers, and umpires involved in insurance claims processes, setting strict standards for their conduct, fees, and professional responsibilities. It also modifies the state's disaster recovery housing assistance program, expanding flexibility for homeowners and renters seeking financial support after natural disasters. Additionally, the bill creates a new mechanism for using the Iowa Economic Emergency Fund, allowing the Department of Management to appropriate up to 10% of the fund's maximum balance annually for disaster response and recovery activities, subject to gubernatorial approval. The legislation includes provisions for tax treatment of disaster recovery grants, post-loss assignment of insurance benefits, and establishes detailed procedures for insurance claim appraisals. Criminal and civil penalties are established for violations of the bill's provisions, with potential felony charges for serious infractions. The bill aims to improve the state's resilience and response capabilities during and after natural disasters while providing more structured support for affected citizens.

bills.title: AZ HB2268 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a Wildfire Prevention Authority to address wildfire risks in Arizona, creating a diverse 10-member board appointed by the governor that includes representatives from insurance companies, state agencies, counties, cities, fire districts, and the general public. The authority will be responsible for reviewing fire risk data, identifying areas most susceptible to wildfire damage, and prioritizing funding for counties, cities, towns, and fire districts that have adopted building or land use codes to reduce wildfire risks. A new Wildfire Prevention Authority Fund will be established, initially funded with $20 million annually from insurance premium taxes, which can be used to make grants for activities such as vegetation management, community hardening projects, infrastructure improvements, and technical assistance to residents and local governments. The authority will have the power to develop operational and financial plans, solicit donations, and will be required to conduct an audit every two years. Grants from the fund can support projects like removing fire fuels, helping low-income and elderly residents create defensible spaces, modifying infrastructure to improve wildfire response, developing local wildfire resilience policies, and providing technical assistance to residents for making their properties more fire-resistant. The bill emphasizes that these funds are meant to supplement, not replace, existing wildfire prevention and mitigation resources.

bills.title: MN SF1758 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends the Minnesota Insurance Guarantee Association (MIGA) rules by expanding the board of directors' ability to request financial information from insureds when evaluating claims related to insolvent insurers. Specifically, the bill allows the board to request financial details from insureds to determine whether their net worth exceeds $25,000,000, which would disqualify them from making a claim through the guarantee association. If an insured fails to provide the requested financial information within 60 days of the board's request, their net worth will be automatically deemed to exceed the $25,000,000 threshold, effectively preventing them from making a claim. The board must inform insureds about the consequences of not providing the requested financial information. This change helps MIGA more accurately assess claims and prevent high-net-worth entities from accessing the insurance guarantee fund, which is designed to protect policyholders of insolvent insurers with more limited financial resources.

bills.title: CO HB1009 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill addresses wildfire prevention by empowering fire protection districts and metropolitan districts to establish voluntary vegetative fuel mitigation programs on privately owned real property within their jurisdictions. The bill defines "vegetative fuel" as dead plant material that can burn, including leaves, grass, shrubs, ground litter, and fallen pine needles. Under the proposed program, districts can require property owners to remove potentially hazardous vegetation through a structured process that includes written notices, potential fines, and opportunities for appeal. The fines, which cannot exceed $200 per incident or $1,200 total per property, must be used to help remove vegetative fuel, with priority given to assisting low-income, senior, or disabled property owners. The bill emphasizes a collaborative approach, requiring districts to provide information about available grant programs, coordinate with local entities, and follow wildfire mitigation codes. Districts must also adopt transparent policies through public hearings and provide multiple notification channels. The legislation aims to reduce wildfire risks by creating defensible spaces around homes and properties, recognizing Colorado's increasingly severe and year-round wildfire threat.

bills.title: UT HB0023 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill makes numerous technical modifications to Utah's insurance laws, focusing on several key areas. The bill updates provisions related to captive insurance companies by expanding definitions, clarifying formation and operational requirements, and providing more detailed regulations for different types of captive insurers, including sponsored captive insurance companies, pure captive insurance companies, and special purpose financial captive insurance companies. It adjusts capital and surplus requirements, investment guidelines, and reporting standards for these entities. The bill also modifies stop-loss insurance standards, increasing the specific attachment point for small employer stop-loss insurance contracts from $10,000 to $25,000 and raising the aggregate attachment point from 85% to 90% of expected claims. Additionally, the bill makes various administrative changes to insurance licensing, including updating language around title insurance producers, clarifying licensing requirements, and modifying disciplinary procedures. Other provisions include allowing insurance policies to be provided in languages other than English while maintaining that the English version will control in case of disputes, and making technical corrections to definitions and procedural requirements across multiple sections of Utah's insurance code. The bill is designed to provide more precise regulatory oversight and operational flexibility for insurance entities while maintaining consumer protections.

bills.title: UT HB0068 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill makes several amendments to Utah state law related to insurance funds and public agency insurance mutuals. The bill introduces and defines new terms such as "public agency insurance mutual" and "reserve fund" in various sections of Utah code. A key provision allows public agency insurance mutuals and reserve funds to form captive insurance companies and authorizes the state treasurer to invest their assets with a primary goal of providing stability, income, and growth of principal. The bill also specifies that the state treasurer should invest and manage these assets as a prudent investor would, considering the purposes and circumstances of the investments while exercising reasonable care, skill, and caution. Additionally, the bill exempts public agency insurance mutuals and reserve funds from certain state money management regulations and provides clarity on their investment standards and governmental status. The amendments also adjust various technical definitions and investment parameters across different sections of Utah law, with the bill set to take effect on May 7, 2025.

bills.title: KS HB2087 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill modifies Kansas insurance regulations by updating the rules for nonadmitted insurers (insurance companies not traditionally authorized to do business in the state) and establishing clearer guidelines for their operations. The bill increases the minimum capital requirement for nonadmitted insurers from $4.5 million to $15 million and requires the insurance commissioner to maintain a comprehensive list of eligible nonadmitted insurers that meet specific financial and operational standards. For vehicle dealers, the bill now allows them to obtain insurance from either traditional authorized insurers or eligible nonadmitted insurers. Excess lines insurance agents (who specialize in placing coverage with nonadmitted insurers) must now obtain written consent from insureds before placing coverage with a nonadmitted insurer, provide detailed disclosures about the insurer's status, and maintain specific records. The bill also provides protection for excess lines agents by stating they are not liable if they have followed proper procedures when placing insurance with a nonadmitted insurer that subsequently becomes insolvent. Additionally, the bill aligns Kansas regulations with federal standards for nonadmitted insurers and removes potential liability for the insurance commissioner in maintaining the insurer list, provided it is done in good faith.

bills.title: IL HB3073 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Predisaster Flood Resilience Grant Program, which will be administered by the Illinois Department of Natural Resources to help flood-prone communities reduce their flood risks and improve their resilience. The program offers two types of grants: assessment grants (up to $300,000) and implementation grants (up to $250,000), with both types covering up to 75% of a project's total cost. Assessment grants will support activities like conducting flood vulnerability assessments, developing culvert inventories, and creating hydrologic models, while implementation grants will fund projects that restore natural water systems, such as reconnecting streams and floodplains or mitigating erosion hazards. Eligible applicants include local governmental units, nonprofit organizations, and private consulting firms, with priority given to areas that have experienced recent flooding disasters or have approved hazard mitigation plans. Grant recipients must complete their projects within two years and submit a comprehensive report detailing their project's goals, activities, and progress. The Department will evaluate grant applications based on factors like flood history, cost-effectiveness, potential measurable results, and overall community benefit, ensuring that the most impactful flood resilience projects receive funding.

bills.title: VT H0466 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill proposes to establish a comprehensive flood response strategy for Vermont, focusing on two key programs designed to help homeowners in flood-prone areas. First, the Agency of Commerce and Community Development would create a voluntary buyout program targeting properties that do not qualify for Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) buyouts or have been identified by municipalities as needing flood-resilient redevelopment or floodplain restoration. Second, the bill would implement a homeowner assistance program providing financial support for several critical flood mitigation efforts, including elevating homes in flood-prone areas, helping displaced residents secure permanent housing, and rebuilding homes using flood-resistant materials and design techniques. By offering these voluntary programs, the bill aims to increase community resilience, reduce future flood damage risks, and provide meaningful support to homeowners impacted by flooding in Vermont.

bills.title: ID HCR016 (2025)
bills.summary: This concurrent resolution addresses the challenges in Idaho's property insurance market by authorizing the Legislative Council to appoint a committee to study the issue. The resolution highlights significant concerns, including nearly a million acres burned in 2024, almost 200 structures destroyed, and twenty-two insurance companies submitting block non-renewal requests. The legislative findings note that property insurance rates are increasing, homeowners are struggling to find coverage (especially in rural areas), home values and repair costs have risen dramatically, and reinsurance costs have become challenging. The authorized committee will explore strategies to stabilize the property insurance market and develop approaches to make property insurance more accessible and affordable for Idahoans. The committee will be composed of legislators determined by the Legislative Council, with the option to include nonlegislative members who will not receive reimbursement or have voting privileges. The committee is required to report its findings, recommendations, and potential proposed legislation to the Second Regular Session of the Sixty-eighth Idaho Legislature, with the ultimate goal of addressing the state's property insurance market challenges.

bills.title: AL SB73 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill modifies Alabama's existing law on Catastrophe Savings Accounts (CSAs) by expanding the allowable expenses that can be covered. Previously, these accounts were primarily designed to cover insurance deductibles for hurricane, flood, or windstorm damage, but the bill now permits CSA funds to be used for additional purposes such as paying for FORTIFIED endorsements (a specific type of insurance coverage that provides enhanced protection for properties) and implementing mitigation actions to reduce potential damage from natural disasters. The bill increases contribution limits, allowing individuals to contribute up to $15,000 in addition to their existing deductible-based contribution limits. The legislation also clarifies the tax treatment of these accounts, maintaining the existing provisions that provide state income tax deductions for contributions and tax exemptions for account earnings. The expanded provisions aim to give Alabama residents more financial flexibility in preparing for and recovering from catastrophic weather events by allowing them to save and use funds for a broader range of property protection and disaster preparedness expenses. The changes will take effect on October 1, 2025, giving taxpayers and financial institutions time to adapt to the new rules.

bills.title: RI S0604 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill creates a comprehensive framework for a home hardening program called "Strengthen Rhody Homes" within the Rhode Island Department of Business Regulation. The program aims to help homeowners make their properties more resilient to hurricane and catastrophic windstorm damage by providing grants for home retrofitting. Key provisions include establishing eligibility requirements for homeowners and contractors, creating a revolving fund to support the program, and mandating that homes be evaluated and upgraded to meet specific insurance industry standards like the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety (IBHS) Fortified Homes Program. The bill also increases the state guaranty fund limits on personal and commercial property insurance claims to one million dollars for first-party covered claims occurring after January 1, 2026. Additionally, the legislation repeals an existing commission on hurricane loss projection methodology and requires insurers to provide premium discounts for homes that meet certain construction standards. The program is not an entitlement and is subject to securing federal or other grant funding, with priority given to lower-income applicants and those in areas more susceptible to catastrophic weather events.

bills.title: NM SB81 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill creates the New Mexico Property Insurance Program Association Board to administer the Fair Access to Insurance Requirements (FAIR) Plan, which provides property insurance for individuals and businesses unable to obtain coverage in the regular market. The board will consist of nine members appointed by various state officials, including representatives from insurance companies, consumer advocacy groups, and experts in areas like actuarial science and climate science. The board will establish a FAIR plan with specific requirements, including setting premium rates that are actuarially sound and not discriminatory, and establishing coverage limits up to $1 million for property and $5 million for commercial property. The bill mandates that FAIR plan policies can only be sold through member insurers and require evidence of at least three coverage declinations. The board can collect fees from member insurers to maintain the program's solvency, and insurers can recoup these fees through policyholder surcharges. The superintendent of insurance will oversee the board, with the power to enforce compliance, impose fines, or revoke insurance licenses for non-compliant insurers. The bill also includes a $50 million appropriation to support the program's administration and fire mitigation efforts, and it includes an emergency clause to implement the act immediately.

bills.title: KS SB121 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill updates and modifies various provisions related to insurance regulation in Kansas, with several key provisions. The bill authorizes the insurance commissioner to select and announce the version of insurance calculations, instructions, and documents that will be in effect for the upcoming calendar year, which must be published in the Kansas register by December 1st. The bill adopts provisions from the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) Holding Company System Regulatory Act, specifically relating to group capital calculations and liquidity stress testing. It expands regulatory oversight by allowing the commissioner to examine insurers and their affiliates to assess financial conditions and enterprise risk, and introduces new requirements for reporting group capital calculations and liquidity stress test results. The bill also updates definitions and exemptions for various insurance entities, clarifies confidentiality and information-sharing protocols for regulatory documents, and establishes guidelines for transactions between insurers and their affiliates. Additionally, the bill modifies definitions related to health benefit plans and health insurers, and provides more detailed explanations of terms like emergency medical conditions and participating providers. Overall, the bill aims to enhance insurance regulation, improve financial transparency, and provide the insurance commissioner with more comprehensive tools for monitoring and assessing insurance companies' financial health.

bills.title: IA HSB246 (2025)
bills.summary: This Study Bill enacts several provisions related to disaster recovery and insurance regulation, including the creation of a Natural Hazard Mitigation Financing Program (Program) administered jointly by the Department of Homeland Security and Emergency Management (HSEMD) and the Iowa Finance Authority (IFA) to provide loans for hazard mitigation projects, and modifies the Disaster Recovery Housing Assistance Program to allow for financial assistance to homeowners and renters without requiring registration in a separate advocacy program. It also establishes that certain disaster recovery new housing grants will not be counted as income for tax purposes, introduces regulations for "post-loss assignments of benefits" by residential contractors to ensure clarity and protect insured parties, and significantly overhauls the licensing and regulation of public, independent, and staff adjusters, as well as appraisers and umpires, by establishing licensing requirements, standards of conduct, and penalties for violations, including felony charges for unlicensed activity or significant financial misconduct. Furthermore, the bill allows for up to 10% of the Iowa Economic Emergency Fund to be appropriated for disaster response and recovery activities at the governor's direction and makes specific appropriations from this fund for nuisance property remediation and disaster recovery housing assistance for the 2024-2025 fiscal year.

bills.title: NC S269 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill revises the Insurance Guaranty Association Act to update several key provisions related to insurance claims and insurer insolvency. The bill expands the definition of "covered claims" to include insurance policies that have been transferred, merged, or assumed by other insurers under specific conditions, and introduces a new definition for "cybersecurity insurance" that covers first and third-party losses related to data breaches, computer viruses, and similar cyber risks. The legislation increases the Association's powers by allowing it to investigate, adjust, and settle claims more flexibly, including the ability to appoint legal counsel and pay claims in any order deemed reasonable. The bill also provides the Association with broader rights to review and contest settlements, releases, and judgments made by insolvent insurers, including the ability to challenge settlements made within 120 days prior to liquidation or those entered through default, fraud, or collusion. Additionally, the bill modifies provisions about net worth calculations for large insureds and clarifies the Association's obligations, including a specific cap of $500,000 for cybersecurity insurance claims arising from a single insured event. The changes aim to provide more clarity and flexibility in handling insurance claims when an insurer becomes insolvent, with a particular focus on modern cybersecurity-related insurance scenarios.

bills.title: NC S276 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill directs the North Carolina Insurance Underwriting Association (NCIUA) and the Joint Underwriting Association to conduct a comprehensive study exploring two potential improvements to the state's property insurance market mechanisms in response to increasing challenges from natural disasters. Specifically, the associations will investigate the feasibility of creating an excess property coverage option, which would provide additional insurance coverage beyond a primary policy to help insureds manage higher-cost losses, and exploring post-event catastrophe bonds, which are specialized debt instruments that could transfer insurance risk to investors or the state in the event of a named storm. The study aims to develop innovative financial strategies to help manage substantial insurance losses from natural disasters. The associations are required to complete the study and submit their findings, including any legislative recommendations, to the House of Representatives Insurance Committee and the Senate Commerce and Insurance Committee by March 1, 2026. The bill becomes effective immediately upon becoming law, signaling the state's proactive approach to addressing potential gaps in property insurance coverage for catastrophic events.

bills.title: NY S00186 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Insure Our Communities Act, a comprehensive legislation aimed at addressing climate change risks and insurance affordability in New York State. The bill requires insurers with over $100 million in annual premiums to report on their investments in fossil fuel industries, their financed and insured emissions, and their climate risk mitigation strategies. Insurance companies must align their investment and underwriting activities with science-based climate targets and will be prohibited from underwriting new fossil fuel projects within five years. The legislation also protects communities vulnerable to "bluelining" - a practice of denying or limiting insurance in certain geographic areas, particularly disadvantaged communities. The bill mandates that insurers cannot refuse to issue or renew policies solely based on a policyholder residing in a disadvantaged community and requires the Department of Financial Services to conduct a study on methods to keep property and casualty insurance affordable for these communities. Additionally, the bill expands reporting requirements for insurers, including detailed demographic and geographic data about policy issuance, claims, and rates, with the goal of identifying and preventing discriminatory insurance practices and promoting climate resilience.

bills.title: WY HB0192 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a comprehensive framework for wildfire mitigation and liability for electric utilities in Wyoming. It requires electric utilities to develop detailed wildfire mitigation plans that must include descriptions of service territories, infrastructure inspection procedures, vegetation management standards, potential facility upgrades, de-energization protocols, system restoration procedures, and community outreach efforts. The bill mandates that these plans be submitted to the Public Service Commission for approval every five years, with the commission evaluating whether the plans are reasonable and balance implementation costs with wildfire risks. The legislation also significantly limits electric utilities' liability in wildfire-related civil actions, stipulating that plaintiffs can only recover economic losses if they can prove the utility either failed to substantially comply with its approved mitigation plan or acted with gross negligence, malice, or criminal intent. Additionally, the bill sets a four-year statute of limitations for filing wildfire damage claims and requires good-faith negotiations between utilities and landowners before any civil action can be initiated. The bill aims to provide electric utilities with clearer guidelines for wildfire prevention while offering some legal protections, ultimately seeking to balance utility responsibilities with practical risk management strategies.

bills.title: IA SSB1188 (2025)
bills.summary: This Study Bill establishes a Natural Hazard Mitigation Financing Program, managed jointly by the Department of Homeland Security and Emergency Management (HSEMD) and the Iowa Finance Authority (IFA), to provide loans for projects that reduce the impact of natural hazards, funded through a revolving loan fund and potentially bonds or notes. It also modifies the Disaster Recovery Housing Assistance Program to allow the governor's disaster proclamation to specify eligibility for homeowners, renters, or both, and removes the requirement for individuals to register with a disaster case advocacy program to receive assistance. Additionally, the bill provides a tax exemption for certain disaster recovery new housing grants awarded between August and December 2024, and introduces regulations for post-loss assignments of benefits by residential contractors, including specific disclosure requirements and cancellation rights for homeowners, while also establishing licensing and regulation for public, independent, and staff adjusters, appraisers, and umpires, with provisions for their conduct, financial responsibility, and penalties for violations, including felony charges for unlicensed practice or willful violations. Finally, the bill allows up to 10% of the Iowa Economic Emergency Fund to be appropriated by the governor for disaster response and recovery activities, with specific appropriations made for FY 2024-2025 to the nuisance property remediation assistance fund and the disaster recovery housing assistance program fund.

bills.title: AL HB106 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill expands the scope of Catastrophe Savings Accounts (CSAs), which are special savings accounts that allow Alabama taxpayers to set aside money for specific property-related expenses. The bill broadens the allowable uses of these accounts beyond just insurance deductibles to now include three types of "Qualified Catastrophe Expenses": (1) insurance deductibles for hurricane, flood, or windstorm damage, (2) the annual cost of a FORTIFIED endorsement (a special insurance designation for storm-resistant properties), and (3) mitigation actions that reduce risk from such events, such as reroofing or obtaining evaluation services for a FORTIFIED designation. The bill increases contribution limits, allowing taxpayers to contribute up to $15,000 in addition to their existing deductible-based limits. Distributions from the account remain tax-exempt if used for these qualified expenses, and the bill maintains existing provisions about tax treatment, including penalties for non-qualifying withdrawals. The changes will apply to tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2026, with the act becoming effective on October 1, 2025, and are designed to help Alabama residents better prepare for and recover from catastrophic weather events.

bills.title: NM HB109 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill creates the Extreme Weather Resilience Fund, a new fund within the state treasury designed to help New Mexico's political subdivisions and Native American communities prepare for and respond to public health emergencies caused by extreme weather and climate impacts. The fund will be initially funded with a $12 million transfer from the state's general fund and will be administered by the state department (likely the Department of Health). Grants of up to $1 million can be awarded to political subdivisions, Indian nations, tribes, or pueblos, with specific prioritization criteria that include: benefiting low-income and disadvantaged communities, integrating public health concerns with climate adaptation and emergency preparedness plans, aligning with potential federal funding opportunities, and involving local and tribal health councils in planning. At least 50% of the grants must be awarded to smaller political subdivisions with populations of 100,000 or fewer. Any unspent money in the fund will revert to the general fund at the end of fiscal year 2028. The fund can accept additional funding through appropriations, gifts, grants, donations, and investment income, providing a flexible mechanism to support climate resilience efforts across the state.

bills.title: MS SB2413 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill renames the "Comprehensive Hurricane Damage Mitigation Program" to the "Strengthen Mississippi Homes Program" and makes several key changes to the state's hurricane and wind damage mitigation efforts. The program will now explicitly include wind damage mitigation, and several previous requirements have been removed, including the cost-benefit study on wind hazard mitigation and the wind certification and hurricane mitigation inspections. The financial grants available to property owners for retrofitting have been increased from $10,000 to $15,000 per recipient, and the Commissioner of Insurance will now have the authority to establish grant eligibility criteria through regulations. The bill also eliminates the previous advisory council associated with the program. Additionally, the bill modifies the funding mechanism by changing the agent certificate of authority fee from $25 to $50, with half of this fee to be deposited into the State General Fund and half into the newly renamed Strengthen Mississippi Homes Fund. The program's goal remains to help property owners retrofit their homes to be less vulnerable to hurricane and wind damage, but with streamlined administrative processes and increased financial support.

bills.title: FL H0013 (2025)
bills.summary:
This bill makes significant changes to windstorm coverage and Citizens Property Insurance Corporation's operations. The bill requires Citizens Property Insurance Corporation to make windstorm coverage available to homeowners for any residential structures starting July 1, 2025, including mobile homes and condominiums. Homeowners can obtain windstorm coverage quotes from insurance agents, with the windstorm portion underwritten by Citizens and potentially administered by the homeowner's chosen property and casualty insurer. The corporation will pool windstorm premiums and use them to settle windstorm claims. 

The bill also modifies several aspects of property insurance regulations, including requirements for hurricane loss projection models, mitigation discounts, deductibles, and reporting requirements for insurers. It updates definitions related to hurricane and windstorm coverage, and makes technical changes to various sections of Florida statutes to conform with the new windstorm coverage provisions. 

Key changes include allowing homeowners more flexibility in obtaining windstorm coverage, establishing new procedures for claims settlement, and ensuring that Citizens Property Insurance Corporation continues to provide an important insurance option for Florida residents. The bill aims to improve the availability and affordability of windstorm insurance while maintaining consumer protections and market stability. The changes will take effect on July 1, 2025, giving insurers and the corporation time to prepare for the new requirements.

bills.title: GA HB606 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill amends Georgia's Fair Access to Insurance Requirements (FAIR) Plan to provide more flexibility in how insurers share risks within the state's property insurance assigned risk pool. Currently, insurers are required to participate in the FAIR Plan and share profits, losses, and expenses proportionally based on their market share for habitational and commercial risks. The bill allows the directors of the FAIR Plan underwriting association, with the Commissioner's approval, to redistribute risks among insurers in a more equitable manner. The legislative findings suggest that this approach may reduce individual insurer exposure, stabilize the FAIR Plan, and discourage insurers from selectively choosing only low-risk policies. Specifically, the bill adds a provision that allows for alternative risk distribution methods beyond the current market share-based approach, while maintaining that if an equitable redistribution is not implemented, risks will continue to be distributed according to the original market share formula. The changes will take effect on January 1, 2026, and are designed to provide more flexibility and potentially more stability to the state's property insurance market for high-risk properties that cannot obtain coverage through traditional private insurance channels.

bills.title: FL H1141 (2025)
bills.summary:
This bill proposes comprehensive changes to insurance regulations in Florida, covering various aspects of insurance oversight, continuing care facilities, reciprocal insurers, and management companies. Here's a summary of the key provisions:

This bill makes extensive modifications to insurance regulations, including establishing new requirements and oversight mechanisms for various types of insurance providers. It introduces more stringent reporting and financial transparency requirements for continuing care facilities, management companies, and reciprocal insurers. The bill creates new standards for financial reporting, cybersecurity, and operational practices across different types of insurance entities. Key provisions include requiring rate transparency reports for property insurers, establishing new reporting requirements for residential property and automobile insurers, and creating more robust oversight mechanisms for management companies and continuing care facilities. The bill also introduces new rules for subscriber contributions and savings accounts for reciprocal insurers, enhances consumer protection measures, and provides the Office of Insurance Regulation with expanded powers to investigate and address potential financial risks in insurance providers. Additionally, the bill implements stricter requirements for management company certifications, financial reporting, and governance, with provisions designed to protect residents and ensure the financial stability of continuing care facilities. Most of these changes will take effect on July 1, 2025, with some specific provisions having different implementation timelines.

bills.title: IL SB2472 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill amends the Illinois Insurance Code to modify notice requirements for insurance policy cancellations and introduce new climate risk disclosure regulations. The bill extends cancellation notice periods, requiring insurers to mail cancellation notices at least 30 days prior to the effective date for the first 60 days of coverage, and at least 60 days prior for coverage lasting more than 60 days. It creates a new Climate Risk Disclosure Article that applies to insurance companies licensed in Illinois under Classes 2 and 3 that write $100 million or more in annual premiums. The new article requires these insurers to participate in climate surveys issued by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) or the Illinois Department of Insurance when directed. The purpose of this new article is to increase transparency about how insurers manage climate-related risks and provide the Department of Insurance with clear authority to require companies to complete climate-related surveys. The monetary threshold for applicability can be adjusted by rule, allowing flexibility in the regulation's scope. Additionally, the bill ensures that cancellation notices must include specific explanations for the cancellation and allows for electronic notifications to mortgagees or lien holders.

bills.title: NH HB106 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes a multi-member commission to comprehensively study and assess the potential financial impacts of climate change on New Hampshire over the next 20 and 50 years. The commission will consist of 9 members, including legislators from both the House and Senate, representatives from state agencies and business/environmental sectors, who will investigate and estimate the likely climate damage scenarios affecting the state's infrastructure, agriculture, tourism, natural resources, and other key industries. The commission's mandate includes determining the scope of potential climate-related damages, identifying necessary adaptation measures, calculating financial estimates for prevention and response strategies, and exploring various methods of recouping associated costs, such as municipal bonding, insurance, legal action, and fees. Members will be appointed by legislative leadership and the governor, with legislative members receiving mileage reimbursement, and the commission will be required to produce a comprehensive report with findings and potential legislative recommendations by November 1, 2026. The bill emphasizes the state's recognition of significant climate-related financial risks and the need for a proactive, equitable approach to addressing potential damages.

bills.title: IL SB1790 (2025, 2026)
bills.summary: This bill creates the Extreme Weather Recovery Act, which establishes a legal mechanism for individuals, businesses, and associations to sue fossil fuel companies for damages caused by climate-related disasters. Specifically, the bill allows harmed parties to bring civil actions against "responsible parties" (fossil fuel companies that emitted at least one billion metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent between 1965 and the present) for climate disasters and extreme weather events. To qualify, plaintiffs must have suffered at least $10,000 in damages, and the lawsuit must be filed within three years of the cause of action. The bill includes extensive legislative findings that outline how fossil fuel companies knew about climate change risks as early as the 1950s but continued to spread misinformation and prioritize profits. The Act provides for strict liability, allowing plaintiffs to seek compensatory and punitive damages, and explicitly prevents state or local government entities from bringing such actions. The bill also includes robust severability provisions to ensure that if any part of the law is found unconstitutional, the remainder would remain in effect. The legislation aims to create a judicial forum for individuals to recover damages related to climate change impacts, emphasizing the state's compelling interest in protecting its citizens from climate-related harm.

bills.title: MS HB1173 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill renames the Comprehensive Hurricane Damage Mitigation Program to the Strengthen Mississippi Homes Program, expanding its scope to include wind mitigation alongside hurricane damage prevention. The legislation removes several previous program components, including a cost-benefit study on wind hazard mitigation, wind certification and hurricane mitigation inspections, and an advisory council. Instead, the bill increases financial grants from $10,000 to $15,000 per home and allows the Commissioner of Insurance to establish grant criteria through regulations. The bill also authorizes the Department of Insurance to enter into personal and professional service contracts to implement the program.
