Additionally, the legislation revises the distribution of nonadmitted policy fees, mandating that beginning July 1, 2025, $10 million will be annually diverted to the Strengthen Mississippi Homes Program Fund, with specific allocations for other funds like the First Responders Health and Safety Trust Fund and the Annual Fire Fund. The bill maintains the program's voluntary nature, emphasizing that it does not create an entitlement for property owners and is subject to legislative funding availability. The changes aim to provide more flexible support for homeowners seeking to improve their properties' resilience against wind and hurricane damage.

bills.title: MS HB1328 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill renames the Comprehensive Hurricane Damage Mitigation Program to the Strengthen Mississippi Homes Program, expanding its focus to include wind mitigation in addition to hurricane damage prevention. The bill increases the maximum financial grant for property retrofitting from $10,000 to $15,000 per home and gives the Commissioner of Insurance more flexibility in establishing grant criteria and program administration. It removes several existing provisions, including the requirement for a cost-benefit study on wind hazard mitigation, wind certification and hurricane mitigation inspections, and the advisory council. The bill also modifies the funding mechanism by redirecting a portion of nonadmitted policy fees to the new Strengthen Mississippi Homes Program Fund starting July 1, 2025, and adjusts the agent certificate of authority fee to partially fund the program. Additionally, the bill makes changes to the Mississippi Insurance Underwriting Association's board of directors, including reducing the number of board members and requiring the board to get the Commissioner of Insurance's advice and consent before removing the executive director. The program's goal remains to help property owners reduce potential damage from hurricanes and wind events, but with streamlined administration and increased funding potential.

bills.title: MS SB2416 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill extends the sunset date for the Comprehensive Hurricane Damage Mitigation Program within the Mississippi Department of Insurance from July 1, 2025, to July 1, 2029. The program, which is not an entitlement and depends on legislative funding, aims to reduce hurricane damage in Mississippi through several key initiatives. These include conducting cost-benefit studies on wind hazard mitigation construction techniques, offering wind certification and hurricane mitigation inspections for residential and commercial properties, providing financial grants (up to $10,000) for property retrofitting, developing public education campaigns about mitigation strategies, and establishing an advisory council with representatives from insurance, construction, and government sectors. The program allows for third-party administration, with administrative costs limited to 1% of appropriated funds, and maintains a special state treasury fund to support its activities. By extending the program's sunset date, the legislature ensures continued support for hurricane damage prevention and mitigation efforts in Mississippi for an additional four years.

bills.title: MS HB1479 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill revises hurricane deductibles for homeowners' insurance policies in Mississippi, effective January 1, 2026. Specifically, the bill defines key terms such as "hurricane" (a storm system declared a hurricane by the National Hurricane Center) and "named storm" (a storm system declared a named storm by the National Hurricane Center). For homeowners' insurance policies covering one- or two-family owner-occupied properties, the bill mandates that hurricane or named storm deductibles will be applied on an annual basis during the calendar year. If an insured experiences multiple named storm or hurricane losses in a single year, the insurer can apply a deductible equal to either the remaining amount of the separate deductible or the deductible for all other perils, whichever is greater. The bill also requires insurers to provide a separate form detailing the specific hurricane, named storm, or wind and hail deductibles, which must be signed by the policyholder. This form is for informational purposes only and does not affect the policy's terms. The bill aims to provide clarity and consistency in how hurricane-related insurance deductibles are applied, helping homeowners understand and plan for potential storm-related losses.

bills.title: KS SB25 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Insurance Savings Account Act, which allows individuals and corporations to create specialized savings accounts designed to help pay for insurance-related expenses starting January 1, 2026. Individuals can contribute up to $6,000 annually ($12,000 for married couples filing jointly), while corporations can contribute up to $25,000 per year. These accounts can be used to pay insurance premiums or cover insurance deductibles for property and casualty insurance. Account holders must maintain documentation of eligible expenses and can invest the funds in various financial products like certificates of deposit. If funds are withdrawn for purposes other than authorized insurance expenses, the withdrawn amount will be subject to tax recapture. The bill also provides tax modifications, allowing account holders to subtract contributions to these accounts from their Kansas adjusted gross income and excluding income earned from the accounts. Financial institutions are not required to track the use of funds or ensure compliance, and the state insurance commissioner may market the program. The accounts can remain open indefinitely without penalty, and in the event of the account holder's death, the funds will be distributed according to the designated beneficiary or probate rules.

bills.title: CT HB06143 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill requires the Insurance Department, working together with the Commissioner of Agriculture, to conduct a comprehensive feasibility study about creating a captive insurance company specifically designed to help farmers who suffer financial losses due to severe weather events. A captive insurance company is a type of self-insurance arrangement where an organization creates its own insurance subsidiary to manage and finance its risk. The bill mandates that by February 1, 2026, the Insurance Department must submit a report to the legislative committee responsible for insurance matters, detailing their recommendations about potentially establishing such a captive insurance company. The goal of this study is to explore a potential innovative financial protection mechanism for farmers who are vulnerable to weather-related economic disruptions, which could provide more targeted and responsive support than traditional agricultural insurance options. The bill does not itself create the insurance company, but rather initiates a formal process to thoroughly investigate its feasibility and potential benefits.

bills.title: ID H0017 (2025)
bills.summary: This bill establishes the Idaho Wildfire Risk Mitigation and Stabilization Pool, an independent public body designed to help homeowners and property owners protect against wildfire risks. The pool will be governed by a 12-member board appointed by the insurance director and will include representatives from insurers, producers, fire officials, and consumer interests. Its primary goals include reducing property insurance costs, mitigating community-level wildfire risks, and promoting a stable insurance marketplace. The pool will be funded through various sources, including premium tax appropriations, cigarette ignition prevention fund allocations, stamping fees, grants, and potential participation charges or assessments from insurers. It will have the authority to enter into contracts, provide grants for wildfire risk mitigation, fund property protection efforts, and support fire prevention education, while ensuring that any programs it develops are actuarially sound and do not compete with the private sector. The bill also provides immunity for board members and participating insurers, protecting them from liability except in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct. The pool will operate under a plan of operation approved by the insurance director and is set to become effective on July 1, 2025.
